AL.name: Alabama
AL.usury_cap: 8% default rate; payday loans capped at 17.5% per $100 advanced ($500 max loan)
AL.consumer_protection_agency: Alabama Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
key_regulations: Payday lenders must be licensed by the State Banking Department under the Deferred Presentment Services Act.
Maximum payday loan amount is $500 with a 10-31 day term.
Title loan interest rates are not capped and can exceed 300% APR.
The Mini-Code governs consumer credit transactions including installment loans.
AL.consumer_rights_summary: Alabama consumers are protected under the Alabama Mini-Code, which regulates consumer credit sales and loans. Payday lending is legal but regulated, with a maximum loan of $500 and a fee cap of 17.5% per $100. Consumers have the right to file complaints with the State Banking Department or the Attorney General's office.

credit_repair_laws.state_statute: Alabama Deceptive Trade Practices Act (applies to credit repair)
key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide a written contract that clearly discloses all terms, conditions, and cancellation rights before any services are rendered
Prohibits false or misleading representations about the ability to improve credit reports or credit ratings
Requires a three-day cancellation period during which a consumer may cancel the contract without obligation
Prohibits requiring or receiving payment before services are actually delivered to the consumer
Bans credit repair companies from advising consumers to dispute accurate information on credit reports
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Alabama Attorney General Consumer Protection Division

personal_loans.rate_cap: 8% per annum (default rate); parties may agree to higher rates by contract
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans are governed under the Alabama Mini-Code (Ala. Code § 8-19-1 et seq.). Interest rates may exceed the default cap if parties contractually agree. No specific maximum loan amount.

payday_loans.rate_cap: 17.5% per $100 advanced (equivalent to approximately 662.5% APR on a 14-day loan)
payday_loans.notes: Governed by the Deferred Presentment Services Act. Payday lenders must be licensed by the State Banking Department. Loans are limited to $500 with terms between 10-31 days. No statewide database tracking required. Borrowers can only have one outstanding payday loan at a time.

title_loans.notes: Title loan interest rates are unregulated and can exceed 300% APR. Title lending is permitted but not specifically licensed or regulated by the State Banking Department. Governed under general consumer credit laws.

installment_loans.rate_cap: No specific cap on rates; governed by contract and market conditions within Alabama Mini-Code framework
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are regulated under the Alabama Mini-Code (Ala. Code § 8-19-1 et seq.), which governs consumer credit sales and installment transactions. Lenders must comply with disclosure and licensing requirements.

mortgage.notes: Mortgages are regulated under Alabama property law and federal lending standards. Alabama is a non-judicial foreclosure state, allowing lenders to foreclose without court proceedings if the mortgage contains a power of sale clause. Residential mortgages must comply with the Truth in Lending Act (TILA) and the Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA). The Alabama Housing Finance Authority administers state mortgage programs and consumer protections.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) establishes a 36% annual percentage rate (MAPR) cap on consumer credit extended to active duty service members and their dependents. This rate cap applies in Alabama and covers payday loans, title loans, installment loans, and other consumer credit products. The MAPR includes interest and most fees. Creditors must provide specific disclosures and cannot require service members to waive MLA protections.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) provides active duty service members with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, including consumer loans and mortgages. SCRA also provides foreclosure protections, lease termination rights, and a grace period for mortgage payments. Service members must provide military status documentation to creditors to receive SCRA benefits in Alabama.
state_specific_protections: Alabama provides property tax exemptions for certain disabled veterans and the surviving spouses/children of deceased veterans who died in service or from service-connected disabilities
The Alabama Department of Veterans Affairs provides counseling and assistance for veterans facing financial difficulties, including predatory lending situations
Alabama state law does not impose additional rate caps beyond federal MLA protections, so the 36% MAPR limit is the primary state-federal safeguard for active duty service members
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Alabama is a participating state in the VA Home Loan program. The Alabama Housing Finance Authority works with the Department of Veterans Affairs to facilitate VA-backed mortgages. Veterans with VA disability ratings may qualify for property tax exemptions. VA loan foreclosures must comply with Alabama's non-judicial foreclosure procedures, and the VA has specific notification requirements for lenders.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort Rucker (U.S. Army Aviation Center of Excellence, Enterprise, AL); Maxwell Air Force Base (Montgomery, AL); Naval Air Station Pensacola (Pensacola, FL - near Alabama border, serves Alabama residents); Fort Novosel (Fort Rucker area)

complaint_resources.name: Alabama State Banking Department
complaint_resources.description: Regulates payday lenders, credit repair organizations, and other financial service providers. Handles licensing, enforcement, and complaints related to payday loans and deferred presentment services.

complaint_resources.name: Alabama Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Investigates consumer fraud, deceptive practices, and lending violations. Accepts complaints about payday lenders, title loan companies, credit repair scams, and other credit/lending abuses.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about payday loans, title loans, credit repair services, and other consumer financial products. Maintains public complaint database and investigates patterns of abuse.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints about credit repair scams, deceptive lending practices, and predatory loan targeting. Works with state attorneys general on enforcement.

complaint_resources.name: Alabama Department of Veterans Affairs
complaint_resources.description: Provides advocacy and assistance for veterans experiencing financial hardship or predatory lending. Can refer veterans to financial counseling and dispute resolution resources.

statute_links.name: Alabama Mini-Code (Consumer Credit Act)
statute_links.description: Comprehensive framework regulating consumer credit sales, installment loans, and related transactions. Establishes default interest rates, licensing requirements, and consumer protections.

statute_links.name: Alabama Deferred Presentment Services Act
statute_links.description: Governs payday lending, including licensing requirements, maximum loan amounts ($500), fee caps (17.5% per $100), and term limits (10-31 days). Administered by State Banking Department.

statute_links.name: Alabama Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair companies, prohibits upfront fees, requires written contracts, and provides a three-day cancellation period. Bans false advertising about credit improvement.

statute_links.name: Alabama State Banking Department - Payday Lending Rules
statute_links.description: Administrative rules and licensing information for payday lenders operating in Alabama, including application procedures and compliance requirements.

statute_links.name: Alabama Usury Law (Default Interest Rate)
statute_links.description: Establishes the default annual interest rate of 8% for loans where parties do not contractually specify a rate. Applies to personal loans and other consumer credit absent written agreement.

AL.recent_changes: No significant changes to Alabama's lending or credit repair laws in 2024-2025. The regulatory framework remains largely consistent, with the State Banking Department continuing to enforce existing payday lending licensing requirements and the Mini-Code consumer protections.

AK.name: Alaska
AK.usury_cap: No general usury cap for licensed lenders; payday loans capped at $14 per $100 for first $500
AK.consumer_protection_agency: Alaska Attorney General Consumer Protection Unit
AK.licensing_board: Alaska Division of Banking and Securities
key_regulations: Payday loans limited to $500 maximum with fees of $14 per $100 for the first $500.
Licensed lenders exempt from the general usury statute.
The Alaska Small Loans Act regulates loans under $25,000.
Borrowers may have only one outstanding payday loan at a time.
AK.consumer_rights_summary: Alaska allows payday lending with consumer protections including a $500 loan cap, fee limits, and a one-loan-at-a-time rule. The state's Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act prohibits deceptive lending practices. Consumers can file complaints through the Division of Banking and Securities or the Attorney General.

credit_repair_laws.state_statute: Alaska Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act (applies to credit repair)
key_provisions: Credit repair companies must provide written contract before performing services, clearly stating all charges and timeline for results
Prohibition on charging advance fees or collecting payment before promised services are performed
Companies must disclose that consumers have right to dispute inaccurate credit information directly with credit bureaus at no cost
Prohibition on misrepresenting credit repair capabilities or guaranteed results
Company must include Alaska Attorney General's office address and phone number in all advertising and contracts
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Alaska Attorney General Consumer Protection Unit

personal_loans.rate_cap: No specific cap for licensed lenders; regulated under Alaska Small Loans Act for loans under $25,000
personal_loans.notes: Licensed lenders are exempt from general usury statute. Terms and rates are negotiable but must comply with disclosure requirements under Truth in Lending Act (TILA).

payday_loans.rate_cap: $14 per $100 borrowed for the first $500; total fees not to exceed $14 per $100
payday_loans.notes: Alaska Stat. § 06.50.500 et seq. regulates payday loans. Borrowers limited to one outstanding payday loan at a time. No specific term limit in statute, but cooling-off period not mandated. Licensed lenders only.

title_loans.notes: Alaska does not specifically authorize title loans as a regulated product. Vehicle loans are governed by general lending laws and consumer protection statutes.

installment_loans.rate_cap: No specific cap for licensed lenders; Alaska Small Loans Act (Alaska Stat. § 06.50.010 et seq.) applies to loans under $25,000
installment_loans.notes: Governed by Alaska Small Loans Act. Licensed lenders exempt from usury cap. Must comply with TILA and Alaska Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act.

mortgage.notes: Alaska allows both judicial and non-judicial foreclosures. Mortgages regulated under Alaska Stat. § 34 (Mechanics' Liens) and Title 34. Foreclosure can proceed non-judicially if deed of trust includes power of sale. Judicial foreclosure also available. Licensing required for mortgage brokers and loan originators under Alaska Division of Banking and Securities. Interest rates unregulated for mortgages. State law includes homestead exemption protections.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) caps MAPR (Military Annual Percentage Rate) at 36% for covered borrowers (active duty service members and their dependents). The cap applies to payday loans, installment loans, and title loans made to covered borrowers in Alaska. MLA also prohibits mandatory arbitration, requires clear disclosures, and restricts fee structures.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) caps pre-service debts at 6% annual interest for active duty service members. SCRA provides protections against foreclosure, eviction, repossession, and lease termination without court order. Applies to all debts incurred before military service. Service members can request SCRA protections by providing military orders.
state_specific_protections: Alaska Stat. § 26.05.115 provides protections for service members' property in domestic relations proceedings, allowing courts to place restrictions on property transfers without consent
Alaska Property Tax Exemption: Disabled veterans and surviving spouses may qualify for property tax exemption under Alaska Stat. § 29.45.030 et seq.
Alaska honors federal protections under SCRA and MLA; state licensing and enforcement by Alaska Division of Banking and Securities
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Alaska does not restrict VA loans; they are governed by federal VA Loan program guidelines administered by U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs. No state-specific VA loan restrictions or separate housing authority programs identified. Alaska offers Disabled Veteran Property Tax Exemption for veterans with service-connected disabilities rated 50% or higher.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Elmendorf Richardson Joint Base (Anchorage), Fort Wainwright (Fairbanks), Eielson Air Force Base (Fairbanks), Coast Guard Integrated Support Command Alaska (Juneau/Ketchikan areas)

complaint_resources.name: Alaska Division of Banking and Securities
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints regarding licensed lenders, payday loan companies, mortgage brokers, loan originators, and credit repair organizations. Enforces state lending laws and issues licenses.

complaint_resources.name: Alaska Attorney General Consumer Protection Unit
complaint_resources.description: Investigates unfair and deceptive practices in lending and credit. Accepts complaints about payday lenders, credit repair companies, and other financial service providers violating Alaska Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about consumer financial products and services, including lending and credit issues. Investigates violations of federal consumer protection laws including TILA, FCRA, and Military Lending Act.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints about credit repair fraud, deceptive lending practices, identity theft, and other consumer financial scams.

complaint_resources.name: Better Business Bureau - Alaska
complaint_resources.description: Provides complaint resolution services and tracks complaints against lenders, credit repair companies, and financial service providers.

statute_links.name: Alaska Small Loans Act
statute_links.description: Regulates licensed lenders making loans under $25,000, including interest rates, fees, and consumer protections.

statute_links.name: Alaska Payday Loan Regulations
statute_links.description: Specifically regulates payday loans including the $500 maximum loan amount, $14 per $100 fee cap, and one-outstanding-loan-at-a-time requirement.

statute_links.name: Alaska Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Requires credit repair companies to disclose terms, prohibits advance fees, and mandates cancellation rights for consumers.

statute_links.name: Alaska Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act
statute_links.description: Provides general consumer protection against deceptive and unfair practices in consumer transactions, including lending.

statute_links.name: Alaska Mechanics' Liens and Mortgage Law
statute_links.description: Regulates mortgage and foreclosure procedures, including non-judicial and judicial foreclosure options.

statute_links.name: Alaska Division of Banking and Securities - Licensing
statute_links.description: Official licensing and regulatory authority; provides information on licensed lenders and complaint procedures.

statute_links.name: Military Lending Act (Federal)
statute_links.description: Federal law capping MAPR at 36% for covered active duty service members and dependents; applies nationwide including Alaska.

statute_links.name: Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (Federal)
statute_links.description: Federal law providing 6% interest cap on pre-service debts and foreclosure/eviction protections for active duty service members.

AK.recent_changes: No significant changes to Alaska consumer lending or credit repair legislation documented for 2024-2025. The state's payday loan framework (Alaska Stat. § 06.50.500) and Credit Services Organization Act (Alaska Stat. § 45.63) remain the primary regulatory mechanisms, with ongoing enforcement by the Division of Banking and Securities and Attorney General.

AZ.name: Arizona
AZ.usury_cap: 36% APR cap on consumer loans; payday lending banned since 2010
AZ.consumer_protection_agency: Arizona Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
AZ.licensing_board: Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions
key_regulations: Payday lending has been banned since July 2010 when the enabling statute expired.
Consumer lenders must be licensed under the Consumer Lenders Act with a 36% APR cap.
Title loans are legal but regulated with licensing requirements.
The Arizona Consumer Fraud Act provides broad consumer protections against deceptive lending.
AZ.consumer_rights_summary: Arizona banned payday lending in 2010, providing strong consumer protections against high-cost short-term loans. Consumer loans are capped at 36% APR under state law. Residents can file complaints with the Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions or the Attorney General's Consumer Protection Division.

key_provisions: Credit services organizations must provide consumers with a written contract detailing all services, fees, and cancellation terms before any work begins
Prohibition on charging or collecting any fees before services are actually delivered to the consumer
Credit services organizations must obtain a surety bond of at least $25,000 and register with the Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions
Consumers have the right to cancel the contract in writing within 5 business days of signing without penalty or obligation
Credit services organizations are prohibited from misrepresenting their services, making false claims about credit repair, or advising consumers to misrepresent information to credit bureaus
Organizations must maintain separate escrow accounts for consumer funds and cannot commingle funds with business operating accounts
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions

personal_loans.rate_cap: 36% APR
personal_loans.notes: Consumer loans are subject to the 36% APR usury cap under Arizona law. Lenders must be licensed under the Consumer Lenders Act (A.R.S. § 34-312 et seq.). The cap applies to consumer loans, which generally include personal loans for personal, family, or household purposes.

payday_loans.rate_cap: N/A - Banned
payday_loans.notes: Payday lending has been banned in Arizona since July 1, 2010, when the enabling statute (A.R.S. § 34-901 et seq.) expired and was not renewed. This prohibition provides strong consumer protection against high-cost short-term loans. No new payday loan licenses may be issued.

title_loans.notes: Title loans are legal in Arizona but regulated under the Consumer Lenders Act with the same 36% APR usury cap as other consumer loans. Lenders must be licensed with the Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions. Title loans are subject to the same consumer protections as other secured consumer loans.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 36% APR
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans fall under Arizona's consumer loan regulations and are subject to the 36% APR cap under the Consumer Lenders Act (A.R.S. § 34-312 et seq.). Lenders must be properly licensed and comply with all consumer disclosure requirements under federal Truth in Lending Act (TILA) and Regulation Z.

mortgage.notes: Arizona mortgages are regulated under state residential mortgage lending laws (A.R.S. § 34-3701 et seq.). Mortgage lenders and brokers must be licensed with the Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions. Arizona uses non-judicial foreclosure proceedings via deed of trust. Judicial foreclosure is also available. Homeowners have statutory redemption rights and protections under the Arizona Foreclosure Mediation Program (A.R.S. § 33-814). Strict compliance with notice requirements is mandatory.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (10 U.S.C. § 987) applies in Arizona and establishes a 36% Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) cap on covered consumer credit products extended to active-duty service members and their dependents. This includes payday loans, title loans, refund anticipation loans, and certain other short-term credit products. Covered lenders must disclose the MAPR clearly and are prohibited from using arbitration clauses or repossession of essential personal property.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3701 et seq.) provides comprehensive protections for active-duty service members in Arizona, including a 6% interest cap on pre-service debts, stays of eviction and foreclosure proceedings during military service, reduced utility deposit requirements, automobile repossession protections, and lease termination rights under specified conditions. Service members must provide written notice of military service to invoke these protections.
state_specific_protections: Arizona does not impose additional usury restrictions on military lending beyond federal MLA requirements, but the state's 36% APR cap on consumer loans aligns with and reinforces federal MLA protections
Arizona's prohibition on payday lending since 2010 provides enhanced protection to service members by eliminating access to high-cost payday loans that are frequently targeted at military personnel

Service members can file complaints regarding MLA violations with the Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions, the Arizona Attorney General, and the federal Consumer Financial Protection Bureau
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Arizona recognizes VA loans and does not impose state-specific restrictions on VA-backed mortgages beyond standard mortgage regulations. Arizona offers property tax exemptions for certain disabled veterans (100% service-connected disabilities) under A.R.S. § 42-111. The Arizona Department of Housing administers veteran housing assistance programs. Arizona also provides veterans preference in state employment and licensing for certain professions.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Major military installations in Arizona include Luke Air Force Base (largest Air Force base west of the Mississippi), Davis-Monthan Air Force Base in Tucson, Fort Huachuca (Army Intelligence Center), Yuma Proving Ground (Army), and Marine Corps Air Station Yuma. These installations represent significant concentrations of active-duty service members subject to MLA protections.

complaint_resources.name: Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions (DIFI)
complaint_resources.description: Handles licensing, regulation, and complaints against consumer lenders, mortgage lenders, credit services organizations, and other financial institutions. Accepts complaints regarding violations of lending laws, unlicensed lending activity, and unfair lending practices.

complaint_resources.name: Arizona Attorney General - Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Enforces the Arizona Consumer Fraud Act and other consumer protection laws. Accepts complaints regarding deceptive lending practices, credit repair scams, payday lending violations, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices. Maintains a complaint database and pursues enforcement actions.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints regarding consumer financial products and services, including lending and credit products. Maintains a national complaint database and pursues enforcement actions against violations of federal consumer protection laws (TILA, FCRA, FDCPA, MLA, etc.).

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints regarding identity theft, fraud, credit repair scams, and deceptive business practices related to credit and lending. Maintains a national complaint database (Consumer Sentinel) shared with law enforcement.

statute_links.name: Arizona Consumer Lenders Act (A.R.S. § 34-312 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Establishes licensing requirements for consumer lenders, the 36% APR usury cap, and consumer protections for non-mortgage consumer loans.

statute_links.name: Arizona Credit Services Organization Act (A.R.S. § 34-224 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair companies, requires registration and bonding, prohibits upfront fees, and establishes cancellation rights for consumers.

statute_links.name: Arizona Consumer Fraud Act (A.R.S. § 34-440 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Provides broad consumer protections against deceptive, unfair, and unconscionable practices in consumer transactions, including lending and credit services.

statute_links.name: Arizona Mortgage Lender and Broker Regulations (A.R.S. § 34-3701 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Establishes licensing and conduct requirements for mortgage lenders and brokers, establishes disclosure requirements, and provides consumer protections.

statute_links.name: Arizona Foreclosure Mediation Program (A.R.S. § 33-814)
statute_links.description: Requires pre-foreclosure mediation and establishes homeowner rights and protections in residential mortgage foreclosure proceedings.

statute_links.name: Arizona Payday Lending Prohibition (Expired A.R.S. § 34-901)
statute_links.description: The enabling statute for payday lending expired on July 1, 2010, and was not renewed, resulting in a complete ban on payday lending in Arizona.

AZ.recent_changes: No significant changes to core consumer lending and credit repair legislation in 2024-2025. Arizona's payday lending ban remains in effect since 2010, and the 36% APR usury cap continues to govern consumer lending. The state maintains active enforcement through DIFI and the Attorney General's office against unlicensed lending and credit repair violations.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.usury_cap: 17% constitutional usury cap (Amendment 89); payday lending effectively banned
AR.consumer_protection_agency: Arkansas Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
key_regulations: The Arkansas Constitution (Amendment 89) caps interest at 17% above the federal discount rate.
Payday lending is effectively banned under the constitutional usury cap.
Check-cashers must be licensed but cannot offer deferred deposit loans.
The Arkansas Deceptive Trade Practices Act protects consumers against predatory lending.
AR.consumer_rights_summary: Arkansas has one of the strongest usury protections in the nation, with a constitutional cap on interest rates at 17% above the federal discount rate. This effectively bans payday lending in the state. Consumers are further protected by the Deceptive Trade Practices Act and can file complaints with the Attorney General.

credit_repair_laws.state_statute: Arkansas Credit Repair Services Organizations Act
key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide written contracts detailing all services, fees, and cancellation terms before collecting any fees
Prohibition on charging or collecting fees before services are fully performed and results are delivered to the consumer
Right to cancel contract within 5 business days without penalty or obligation
Credit repair companies must disclose that consumers have the right to dispute items directly with credit bureaus for free
Prohibition on misrepresenting or making false claims about ability to remove accurate negative information from credit reports

personal_loans.rate_cap: 17% above the federal discount rate (Ark. Const. Amendment 89)
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans are subject to the constitutional usury cap of 17% above the federal discount rate, which is approximately 20% as of current federal rates. Banks and credit unions may have additional regulations under federal law.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Effectively banned under constitutional usury cap
payday_loans.notes: Payday lending is effectively banned in Arkansas due to the constitutional usury cap (17% above federal discount rate) which makes short-term, high-fee lending economically unfeasible. Check-cashers are licensed but cannot offer deferred deposit transactions.

title_loans.rate_cap: Subject to constitutional usury cap of 17% above federal discount rate
title_loans.notes: Title lending is not explicitly regulated as a distinct product in Arkansas. Any secured lending would be subject to the constitutional usury cap, which effectively prevents the traditional title loan model common in other states.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 17% above the federal discount rate (Ark. Const. Amendment 89)
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are permitted and governed by the constitutional usury cap. Creditors are required to disclose all material terms and comply with the Arkansas Deceptive Trade Practices Act.

mortgage.notes: Mortgages in Arkansas are subject to foreclosure via judicial proceeding (mandatory court process). The state has a 12-month redemption period following foreclosure sale. First-mortgage lenders must be licensed; the Arkansas Securities Department regulates mortgage lending. Arkansas recognizes homestead exemption protecting primary residences from certain creditor claims.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) applies to active-duty service members and their dependents in Arkansas, capping annual percentage rates (MAPR) at 36% for covered borrowing. Covered loans include payday loans, vehicle title loans, and certain installment loans. Lenders must provide clear disclosure of MLA protections and verify military status.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) applies to Arkansas service members, capping interest rates on pre-service debts at 6%, protecting against foreclosure and eviction during active duty, and allowing lease termination under specific conditions. Creditors must verify military status and comply with SCRA notice requirements.
state_specific_protections: Arkansas offers a homestead exemption that provides limited protection of primary residences for certain veteran populations, though this is not exclusively a veteran program
Veterans are eligible for state-administered VA loan guaranty programs through the Arkansas Veterans Commission, which facilitate home purchases with favorable terms
Arkansas does not impose state income tax on military pensions, providing additional financial relief to retired service members
The Arkansas Veterans Commission administers benefits counseling and connects veterans with financial assistance programs
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Arkansas participates in VA loan guarantee programs administered through the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs. The Arkansas Veterans Commission provides counseling and information about VA benefits. Arkansas has no state-specific property tax exemptions exclusively for veterans, but homeowners may qualify for general property tax relief programs.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Major military installations in Arkansas include Fort Liberty (Fort Bragg), located in Cumberland County, North Carolina (outside state), but Arkansas serves as home to Joint Base Barksdale-Shreveport personnel in neighboring Louisiana. Little Rock Air Force Base is located in Pulaski County, Arkansas and represents the primary active federal military installation within the state.

complaint_resources.name: Arkansas Securities Department
complaint_resources.description: Regulates mortgage lenders, check-cashers, and other financial service providers in Arkansas; handles licensing and complaint investigations for lending violations.

complaint_resources.name: Arkansas Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Investigates complaints related to deceptive trade practices, predatory lending, credit repair fraud, and other consumer finance violations. Primary state enforcement agency for the Arkansas Deceptive Trade Practices Act.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal agency handling complaints about credit cards, mortgages, personal loans, payday loans, credit reporting, and other consumer financial products and services.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Investigates complaints about credit repair scams, identity theft, fraudulent lending practices, and other deceptive financial practices affecting Arkansas consumers.

statute_links.name: Arkansas Constitution Amendment 89 (Usury Cap)
statute_links.description: Constitutional amendment establishing the 17% above federal discount rate usury cap and effectively banning payday lending in Arkansas.

statute_links.name: Arkansas Code Annotated § 4-90-101 et seq. (Credit Services Organization Act)
statute_links.description: Statute regulating credit repair companies, requiring written contracts, prohibiting upfront fees, and establishing a 5-business-day cancellation period.

statute_links.name: Arkansas Code Annotated § 4-88-101 et seq. (Deceptive Trade Practices Act)
statute_links.description: Consumer protection statute prohibiting unfair and deceptive practices in lending and credit transactions, providing remedies for affected consumers.

statute_links.name: Arkansas Code Annotated § 34-27-101 et seq. (Check-Casher Licensing)
statute_links.description: Statute establishing licensing requirements for check-cashers and prohibiting them from offering deferred deposit (payday loan) transactions.

statute_links.name: Arkansas Code Annotated § 34-39-101 et seq. (Mortgage Lending)
statute_links.description: Statute regulating mortgage lending, licensing requirements, and foreclosure procedures in Arkansas.

AR.recent_changes: No significant changes to Arkansas consumer lending or credit repair legislation in 2024-2025. The constitutional usury cap (Amendment 89) remains the primary regulatory framework, with enforcement focus continuing on payday lending prohibition and compliance with the Deceptive Trade Practices Act.

CA.name: California
CA.usury_cap: 10% for personal/consumer loans (Article XV, CA Constitution); payday loans capped at $15 per $100
CA.licensing_board: California Department of Financial Protection and Innovation (DFPI)
key_regulations: Payday loans capped at $300 with maximum fee of $15 per $100 (459% APR equivalent).
The California Consumer Financial Protection Law grants DFPI broad enforcement authority.
Licensed finance lenders under the California Financing Law can charge rates above usury for loans under $10,000.
AB 539 (2020) caps APR at 36% plus federal funds rate for loans $2,500-$10,000.
CA.consumer_rights_summary: California regulates payday loans at a maximum of $300 with a $45 fee cap. The DFPI oversees all consumer lending and enforces the California Consumer Financial Protection Law. Consumers have strong rights under the state's comprehensive lending regulations, including the ability to file complaints online with the DFPI.

key_provisions: Credit repair companies must provide a written contract disclosing all terms, conditions, and cancellation rights before any services are performed
Prohibition on making false or misleading statements about the company's ability to improve credit records or remove accurate negative information
Companies cannot charge or collect fees until services are actually delivered and the consumer has received the promised results
Prohibition on advising consumers to misrepresent information or dispute accurate information on credit reports
Companies must disclose the consumer's right to obtain a free credit report annually and explain how to dispute inaccurate information directly with credit bureaus
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: California Department of Financial Protection and Innovation (DFPI)

personal_loans.rate_cap: 10% APR for unlicensed lenders; licensed finance lenders can exceed usury cap for loans under $10,000; AB 539 (2020) caps APR at 36% plus federal funds rate for loans $2,500-$10,000
personal_loans.notes: California Constitution Article XV sets 10% usury cap for general consumer loans. Licensed finance lenders under the California Financing Law (Cal. Fin. Code § 22000 et seq.) can charge rates above the constitutional usury limit for certain loan amounts.

payday_loans.rate_cap: $15 per $100 borrowed (equivalent to 459% APR on typical 14-day loan)
payday_loans.notes: Payday loans capped at $300 with maximum fee of $45 total. One loan at a time allowed. California uses the Statewide Payday Loan Database to prevent rollovers. Regulated under Cal. Fin. Code § 22250 et seq. Lenders must be licensed with DFPI.

title_loans.notes: Title loans and automobile lending secured by vehicle title are prohibited in California under Cal. Fin. Code § 22675. This ban was established to protect consumers from predatory lending practices.

installment_loans.rate_cap: Governed by California Financing Law. Licensed lenders can exceed usury cap for loans under $10,000. AB 539 caps APR at 36% plus federal funds rate for loans $2,500-$10,000.
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans regulated under Cal. Fin. Code § 22000 et seq. Installment Loan Law requires disclosure of finance charge, APR, payment schedule, and other terms. DFPI oversees licensing and enforcement.

mortgage.notes: California mortgages are subject to non-judicial foreclosure (power of sale) under Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 2924 et seq. This is a non-judicial state. Homeowners have statutory right to reinstate or redeem; strict notice requirements apply. Residential mortgage lenders must be licensed with DFPI. California has strong anti-predatory lending protections including restrictions on prepayment penalties (Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1916-3) and requirements for escrow accounts. Dodd-Frank mortgage servicing rules apply federally.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The federal Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) applies to active-duty service members and their dependents in California. The 36% MAPR (Military Annual Percentage Rate) cap covers interest, fees, and most charges on consumer loans. This applies to payday loans, title loans, installment loans, and other covered credit products. Covered borrowers cannot be required to waive rights and protections under the MLA.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3951 et seq.) provides California military personnel with: 6% interest cap on pre-service debts; protection from eviction and foreclosure without court order while on active duty plus 9 months; ability to terminate residential leases with military deployment; and protection from default judgments. SCRA applies to all states including California.
state_specific_protections: California law (Cal. Gov. Code § 8200 et seq.) provides property tax exemptions for disabled veterans, Medal of Honor recipients, and their surviving spouses, reducing property tax burden
Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1170.7 provides additional protections for military service members from unlawful detainer (eviction) actions when deployed
DFPI enforces Military Lending Act compliance by California-licensed lenders; violations can result in license suspension or revocation
California's Homeowner Bill of Rights (Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 580b) protects service members and all borrowers from deficiency judgments on purchase-money mortgages
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: California is a major VA loan state with no state-imposed restrictions on VA loans. The California Department of Veterans Affairs provides state-specific VA benefits information and housing assistance programs. No state property tax cap applies specifically to VA loans, but disabled veteran property tax exemptions are available under California law. Numerous VA Loan Centers operate throughout California in Los Angeles, San Francisco, San Diego, and other major cities.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Major military installations include: Naval Base San Diego, Camp Pendleton (Marine Corps), Naval Air Station North Island, Beale Air Force Base, Edwards Air Force Base, China Lake Naval Air Weapons Station, Fort Irwin, Los Alamitos Armed Forces Reserve Center, and Vandenberg Space Force Base. These bases are primary MLA enforcement focus areas.

complaint_resources.name: California Department of Financial Protection and Innovation (DFPI)
complaint_resources.description: DFPI oversees consumer lending, payday loans, credit repair companies, and other financial services. Accepts complaints about unlicensed lenders, payday loan violations, predatory lending, and consumer financial services violations.

complaint_resources.name: California Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Handles consumer protection complaints including lending fraud, credit repair scams, and violations of California consumer protection laws. Accepts online complaints through the Consumer Complaint Center.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal consumer complaint database covering all consumer financial products including payday loans, installment loans, credit repair services, and mortgages. Complaints are shared with state regulators.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints about credit repair scams, predatory lending, fraud, and identity theft. Maintains identity theft database and coordinates with state enforcers.

complaint_resources.name: California Department of Consumer Affairs
complaint_resources.description: Oversees professional licensing and handles complaints about licensed professionals; provides consumer resources and dispute resolution assistance.

statute_links.name: California Constitution Article XV (Usury)
statute_links.description: Establishes the 10% constitutional usury cap for personal and consumer loans in California.

statute_links.name: California Finance Code § 22000 et seq. (California Financing Law)
statute_links.description: Governs licensed finance lenders, installment loans, and allows rates above usury cap for certain loan amounts.

statute_links.name: California Finance Code § 22250 et seq. (Payday Loan Law)
statute_links.description: Comprehensive payday loan regulations including $300 cap, $15 per $100 fee limit, and database tracking requirements.

statute_links.name: California Finance Code § 22675 (Title Loan Prohibition)
statute_links.description: Prohibits title loans and automobile lending secured by vehicle title in California.

statute_links.name: California Civil Code § 1789.10-1789.26 (Credit Services Organization Act)
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair companies, requires registration with DFPI, prohibits upfront fees, and provides consumer cancellation rights.

statute_links.name: California Finance Code § 4000 et seq. (California Consumer Financial Protection Law)
statute_links.description: Grants DFPI broad enforcement authority over consumer financial services and unlicensed lending.

statute_links.name: Assembly Bill 539 (2020) - 36% APR Cap
statute_links.description: Caps APR at 36% plus federal funds rate for loans between $2,500 and $10,000.

statute_links.name: California Code of Civil Procedure § 2924 et seq. (Non-Judicial Foreclosure)
statute_links.description: Governs non-judicial foreclosure procedures in California with strict notice and timeline requirements.

statute_links.name: California Government Code § 8200 et seq. (Disabled Veteran Property Tax Exemption)
statute_links.description: Provides property tax exemptions for disabled veterans, Medal of Honor recipients, and their surviving spouses.

statute_links.name: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1170.7 (Military Service Member Protections)
statute_links.description: Protects military service members on active duty from eviction actions while deployed.

CA.recent_changes: In 2020, Assembly Bill 539 capped APR at 36% plus the federal funds rate for loans between $2,500 and $10,000, significantly restricting rates on mid-sized personal loans. As of 2024, California continues to strengthen enforcement of payday loan regulations through the Statewide Payday Loan Database and DFPI oversight, with ongoing legislative focus on preventing predatory lending and protecting vulnerable consumers.

CO.name: Colorado
CO.usury_cap: 36% APR cap on payday loans (2018 ballot measure); 12% for consumer loans under usury statute
CO.consumer_protection_agency: Colorado Attorney General Consumer Protection Section
CO.licensing_board: Colorado Department of Regulatory Agencies - Division of Banking
key_regulations: Proposition 111 (2018) capped payday loan APR at 36% and eliminated balloon payments.
The Uniform Consumer Credit Code governs most consumer lending in the state.
Payday loans limited to $500 with a minimum 6-month term.
Supervised lenders licensed by the Division of Banking can charge rates above the usury cap.
CO.consumer_rights_summary: Colorado voters approved Proposition 111 in 2018, capping payday loan APR at 36% and requiring minimum 6-month terms. The Uniform Consumer Credit Code provides comprehensive consumer protections for all credit transactions. Consumers can file complaints with the Attorney General or the Division of Banking.

key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide written contract before any services rendered, with clear explanation of services to be performed and fees charged
All written contracts must include a 3-day cancellation period allowing consumers to terminate without obligation
Organizations are prohibited from charging fees for credit repair services prior to completion and delivery of promised results
Credit repair organizations cannot misrepresent ability to remove accurate negative information or charge consumers for services denied by consumers' refusal to dispute items
Bond requirement of $25,000 or letter of credit required for all credit services organizations
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Colorado Attorney General (Administrator of the Uniform Consumer Credit Code)

personal_loans.rate_cap: 12% APR (Colorado Uniform Consumer Credit Code general usury cap); licensed lenders may charge higher rates with state supervision
personal_loans.notes: Governed by Colorado Uniform Consumer Credit Code (C.R.S. § 5-3.1-101 et seq.). Supervised lenders licensed by Division of Banking may exceed the 12% usury cap.

payday_loans.rate_cap: 36% APR maximum (established by Proposition 111, 2018)
payday_loans.notes: Colorado payday loans are restricted and regulated under C.R.S. § 5-3.1-301 et seq. Proposition 111 eliminated balloon payments, established 36% APR cap, and required minimum 6-month repayment terms. Lenders must participate in the Colorado Consumer Credit Database. Maximum of one outstanding payday loan allowed; 1-day cooling-off period between loans required.

title_loans.notes: Title loans are not explicitly authorized under Colorado law and are effectively prohibited through the absence of enabling legislation. The Uniform Consumer Credit Code and payday loan regulations do not permit title-secured lending.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 12% APR general cap (C.R.S. § 5-3.1-102); supervised lenders may charge higher rates with state authorization
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are governed by the Colorado Uniform Consumer Credit Code (C.R.S. § 5-3.1-101 et seq.). Licensed supervised lenders may charge rates above the 12% usury cap with Division of Banking approval.

mortgage.notes: Colorado mortgages are regulated under the Colorado Residential Mortgage Loan Law (C.R.S. § 12-61-901 et seq.) and the Uniform Consumer Credit Code. Foreclosures are judicial proceedings in Colorado. Lenders must provide proper notice and opportunity for cure. The Colorado Department of Regulatory Agencies - Division of Real Estate regulates mortgage brokers and loan originators. FHA loans are available; VA loans are available and backed by the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The federal Military Lending Act (10 U.S.C. § 987) caps interest rates on covered consumer credit at 36% MAPR (Military Annual Percentage Rate) for active-duty service members and their dependents. This applies nationwide including Colorado. Colorado's 36% payday loan cap aligns with federal MLA requirements. The MLA prohibits mandatory arbitration, certain prepayment penalties, and requirement of military allotments.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3701 et seq.) provides Colorado service members with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, protection against default judgments, lease termination rights, and insurance premium protections during active duty. SCRA applies to all creditors in Colorado; service members must provide military service documentation.
state_specific_protections: Colorado C.R.S. § 12-1-704 provides property tax exemptions for disabled veterans and surviving spouses of military members who died in service or from service-connected disability
Colorado Department of Human Services provides veterans with housing assistance programs and benefits coordination through the Division of Veterans Affairs
Colorado laws prohibit creditors from denying credit or increasing rates based solely on military service status; compliance with MLA is state law requirement
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Colorado participates fully in VA Home Loan programs. The state has no additional VA loan restrictions. Property tax exemptions available for disabled veterans (100% exemption if 100% disabled) and surviving military spouses (100% exemption). Colorado Housing and Finance Authority administers state veteran housing programs.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort Carson (El Paso County), United States Air Force Academy (El Paso County), Buckley Space Force Base (Aurora, Denver County)

complaint_resources.name: Colorado Division of Banking
complaint_resources.description: Handles licensing and supervision of lenders, credit repair organizations, and mortgage loan originators. Accepts complaints regarding payday loans, consumer credit, and lending practices.

complaint_resources.name: Colorado Attorney General - Consumer Protection Section
complaint_resources.description: Enforces Colorado consumer protection laws, handles complaints regarding deceptive lending practices, credit repair fraud, and consumer credit violations. Accepts complaints online and by mail.

complaint_resources.name: Colorado Department of Regulatory Agencies
complaint_resources.description: Oversees Division of Banking and provides regulatory oversight of financial services providers. Can direct complaints to appropriate division.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal consumer financial protection complaints regarding lending practices, credit reporting, debt collection, and other consumer financial products and services.

complaint_resources.name: Colorado Division of Real Estate
complaint_resources.description: Licenses and regulates mortgage brokers and mortgage loan originators in Colorado; handles complaints regarding mortgage lending practices.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints regarding credit repair fraud, identity theft, and deceptive business practices in consumer credit.

statute_links.name: Colorado Uniform Consumer Credit Code
statute_links.description: Comprehensive state law governing consumer credit transactions, interest rates, and lender licensing (C.R.S. § 5-3.1-101 et seq.)

statute_links.name: Proposition 111 (2018) - Payday Loan Regulations
statute_links.description: Statewide ballot measure establishing 36% APR cap on payday loans, 6-month minimum term, elimination of balloon payments, and database tracking requirements

statute_links.name: Colorado Consumer Credit Code - Payday Loans (C.R.S. § 5-3.1-301 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Specific regulations governing payday lending, including caps, terms, cooling-off periods, and database requirements

statute_links.name: Colorado Credit Services Organization Act (C.R.S. § 12-14.3-101 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair organizations, establishes bonding requirements, contract requirements, and prohibitions on upfront fees

statute_links.name: Colorado Residential Mortgage Loan Law
statute_links.description: Comprehensive mortgage lending regulations, licensing requirements for originators and brokers, and foreclosure procedures (C.R.S. § 12-61-901 et seq.)

statute_links.name: Colorado Consumer Protection Act
statute_links.description: General consumer protection law prohibiting deceptive and unfair practices in commerce, including lending and credit services (C.R.S. § 6-1-101 et seq.)

statute_links.name: Colorado Division of Banking Official Website
statute_links.description: Official regulatory agency for banking, lending, and credit services in Colorado; provides lender search, regulations, and complaint procedures

CO.recent_changes: Colorado HB24-1032 (effective 2024) made additional clarifications to payday loan regulations and database tracking requirements. No major legislative changes to the 36% APR payday cap or personal loan usury limits in 2024-2025; Proposition 111 (2018) remains the primary recent reform. Monitor for potential adjustments to consumer credit regulations through the Division of Banking.

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.usury_cap: 12% general usury cap; payday lending banned
key_regulations: Payday lending is prohibited under Connecticut law; no deferred deposit licenses are issued.
The general usury cap is 12% for most consumer loans.
Licensed small loan companies can charge up to 30.03% APR on first $600.
The Connecticut Unfair Trade Practices Act provides consumer protection against predatory lending.
CT.consumer_rights_summary: Connecticut bans payday lending entirely and maintains a 12% general usury cap. The Department of Banking actively regulates consumer lenders and enforces licensing requirements. Consumers have robust protections under the Unfair Trade Practices Act and can file complaints with either the Department of Banking or the Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit services organizations must be registered with the Connecticut Department of Banking before operating in the state.
All contracts must be in writing, clearly disclose all terms, services, and fees, and include a three-day cancellation period.
Upfront fees are prohibited; charges may only be collected after services are actually performed.
Organizations cannot make false or misleading claims about their ability to improve credit scores or remove accurate negative information.
Credit repair companies must maintain a surety bond of $25,000 and provide consumers with a complete disclosure of their rights under the federal Fair Credit Reporting Act.
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Connecticut Department of Banking

personal_loans.rate_cap: 12% APR (general usury cap); licensed small loan companies can charge up to 30.03% APR on loans up to $600
personal_loans.notes: Connecticut's general usury cap of 12% applies to most personal loans. Licensed small loan lenders operating under Conn. Gen. Stat. § 36a-626 are exempt from the general usury cap and may charge higher rates on smaller loan amounts.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Payday lending is prohibited
payday_loans.notes: Connecticut bans payday lending entirely. No deferred deposit licenses are issued. This prohibition is enforced under Connecticut General Statutes and the Department of Banking does not issue any payday lending licenses.

title_loans.rate_cap: Not applicable; title lending is prohibited
title_loans.notes: Connecticut does not permit title lending arrangements. The prohibition aligns with the state's strict consumer lending protections.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 12% APR (general usury cap); licensed small loan companies exempt and may charge up to 30.03% APR on loans under $600
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are regulated under Connecticut General Statutes § 36a-1 et seq. The Department of Banking oversees licensure and compliance. Creditors must comply with disclosure requirements under the Truth in Lending Act (TILA) and Connecticut's regulations.

mortgage.notes: Connecticut mortgages are governed by Conn. Gen. Stat. § 49-1 et seq. and supervised by the Department of Banking. Connecticut allows both judicial and non-judicial foreclosure; however, judicial foreclosure is more commonly used. Lenders must comply with federal mortgage lending laws including TILA, RESPA, Fair Lending Act, and ECOA. All mortgage lenders must be licensed by the Department of Banking.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The federal Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) caps the Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) at 36% for servicemembers and their dependents on consumer credit. This rate cap applies in Connecticut and is enforced by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and the Department of Defense. The MLA also prohibits mandatory arbitration clauses and limits fee restrictions on covered loans.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3951 et seq.) provides servicemembers on active duty with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, protection from foreclosure and eviction during active duty plus 9 months after, lease termination rights, and reduced liability on secured debts. These federal protections apply in Connecticut regardless of state law.
state_specific_protections: Connecticut recognizes the federal Military Lending Act protections and enforces compliance through the Department of Banking's consumer lending oversight.

Connecticut offers property tax exemptions for totally and permanently disabled veterans, including those with service-connected disabilities rated by the VA, which may reduce housing costs for eligible veteran borrowers.
The state provides veterans' home loan benefits through various state-administered programs that complement VA loans and offer favorable financing terms.

Connecticut's Unfair Trade Practices Act (Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42-110 et seq.) provides an additional state-level consumer protection remedy against predatory lending that disproportionately affects military families.
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Connecticut recognizes VA loans under federal guidelines. The state offers additional veterans' home loan programs through the Connecticut Housing Authority, including below-market-rate financing for eligible veterans. Connecticut also provides a property tax credit for totally disabled veterans.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Major military installations in Connecticut include Naval Submarine Base New London (Groton), home to the U.S. Naval Submarine Force Atlantic; Sikorsky Memorial Airport in Stratford (supported military research and development); and the Connecticut Air National Guard facilities. Naval Station Newport, Rhode Island is nearby and serves Connecticut residents.

complaint_resources.name: Connecticut Department of Banking
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints against licensed lenders, credit repair organizations, and mortgage companies. Enforces consumer lending laws, usury regulations, and licensing requirements.

complaint_resources.name: Connecticut Attorney General – Consumer Protection
complaint_resources.description: Investigates unfair or deceptive lending practices and violations of the Connecticut Unfair Trade Practices Act. Accepts complaints about predatory lending, debt collection abuse, and credit repair fraud.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about consumer financial products and services, including lending, credit reporting, debt collection, and credit repair. Provides federal oversight and enforcement.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints about credit repair fraud, deceptive lending practices, and identity theft. Shares information with state regulators for enforcement action.

complaint_resources.name: Connecticut Better Business Bureau
complaint_resources.description: Provides complaint mediation and accreditation information for lenders and credit services organizations operating in Connecticut.

statute_links.name: Connecticut General Statutes § 36a-1 et seq. (Banking Code)
statute_links.description: Comprehensive banking and consumer lending regulations, including usury caps, small loan company licensing, and consumer credit protections.

statute_links.name: Connecticut General Statutes § 36a-700 to 36a-714 (Credit Services Organization Act)
statute_links.description: Regulation of credit repair organizations, registration requirements, bond requirements, contract standards, and consumer rights.

statute_links.name: Connecticut General Statutes § 42-110 et seq. (Unfair Trade Practices Act)
statute_links.description: Prohibits unfair or deceptive trade practices, including predatory lending; provides private right of action for consumers harmed by violations.

statute_links.name: Connecticut General Statutes § 36a-626 (Small Loan Company Licensing)
statute_links.description: Governs licensing and rate caps for small loan lenders; allows 30.03% APR on loans up to $600 in lieu of general 12% usury cap.

statute_links.name: Connecticut Department of Banking – Regulations and Statutes
statute_links.description: Official source for Connecticut banking regulations, licensing requirements, and enforcement guidance.

statute_links.name: Connecticut General Statutes § 49-1 et seq. (Real Property Law – Mortgages)
statute_links.description: Mortgage law and foreclosure procedures; establishes judicial foreclosure as the primary method in Connecticut.

CT.recent_changes: Connecticut has maintained its comprehensive payday lending ban and 12% general usury cap without major statutory changes in 2024-2025. The state continues to enforce strict credit repair organization registration and bonding requirements under § 36a-700 et seq., with active oversight by the Department of Banking.

DE.name: Delaware
DE.usury_cap: No usury cap for licensed lenders (5 Del. C. 2227); payday loans legal with no rate cap
DE.consumer_protection_agency: Delaware Attorney General Consumer Protection Unit
DE.licensing_board: Delaware Office of the State Bank Commissioner
key_regulations: Delaware has no usury cap for licensed lenders, making it one of the most lender-friendly states.
Payday lenders must be licensed under the Delaware Licensed Lenders Act.
Short-term consumer loans have no APR cap, resulting in some of the highest rates in the nation.
The Consumer Fraud Act prohibits deceptive practices in lending.
DE.consumer_rights_summary: Delaware permits payday lending with minimal rate restrictions, as licensed lenders are exempt from usury caps. Consumers should exercise caution as APRs can be extremely high. Complaints can be filed with the Office of the State Bank Commissioner or the Attorney General's Consumer Protection Unit.

key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide clients with a written contract clearly stating all services, fees, and payment terms before any services are performed
Organizations are prohibited from charging upfront fees before services are delivered; fees must be based on actual results
Clients have the right to cancel contracts within 5 business days without penalty or obligation
Credit repair organizations cannot make false or misleading claims about their ability to repair credit or remove accurate negative information
Organizations must disclose consumers' rights regarding credit reporting and the ability to dispute directly with credit bureaus
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Delaware Secretary of State

personal_loans.rate_cap: No specific cap for licensed lenders under 5 Del. C. § 2227; unlicensed lenders subject to 5% above Federal Reserve discount rate
personal_loans.notes: Licensed lenders have no usury restrictions; unlicensed lenders are subject to usury limits. Personal loans are common for credit-qualified borrowers.

payday_loans.rate_cap: No APR cap; fees and interest are unregulated for licensed payday lenders
payday_loans.notes: Delaware permits payday loans with minimal regulatory restrictions. Payday lenders must be licensed under the Delaware Licensed Lenders Act (5 Del. C. § 2201 et seq.). No mandatory waiting periods or database tracking requirements exist. APRs can exceed 400% in practice.

title_loans.rate_cap: No specific cap for licensed lenders
title_loans.notes: Title loans are permitted and regulated under the Delaware Licensed Lenders Act. Licensed lenders face no rate caps; unlicensed lenders subject to general usury provisions.

installment_loans.rate_cap: No specific cap for licensed lenders; unlicensed lenders subject to 5% above Federal Reserve discount rate
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are legal in Delaware. Licensed lenders have no rate restrictions. Regulated under the Delaware Consumer Credit Code (5 Del. C. Chapter 22).

mortgage.notes: Delaware mortgages are regulated under 5 Del. C. Chapter 22 (Consumer Credit Code) and the Delaware Residential Mortgage Licensing Act (5 Del. C. § 2901 et seq.). Foreclosures in Delaware are primarily judicial, requiring court action. Residential mortgage lenders must be licensed. Delaware allows for both purchase-money and non-purchase-money mortgages.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) caps interest rates (including fees) at 36% MAPR for active-duty service members and their dependents. This applies to all covered borrowers in Delaware regardless of state law. Lenders must verify military status and comply with MLA disclosure requirements.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) limits interest on pre-service debts to 6% and provides foreclosure protections, eviction protections, and lease termination rights for active-duty service members. These protections apply in Delaware state courts and supersede state lending law.
state_specific_protections: Delaware recognizes and enforces all federal military lending protections through state courts; no additional state-specific military lending caps exist beyond federal MLA
Delaware Office of the State Bank Commissioner coordinates with federal regulators on military lending compliance monitoring
Service members experiencing financial hardship can access legal assistance through Delaware military legal assistance programs and veterans' organizations
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Delaware is a VA-friendly state for VA home loans with no state-imposed restrictions. The Delaware Housing Authority does not impose additional fees on VA loans. Delaware has property tax exemptions available for disabled veterans (certain percentages based on disability rating). VA loans are exempt from Delaware's foreclosure requirements and receive favorable treatment in state courts.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Dover Air Force Base (Dover) — major installation with significant active-duty and dependent population; various National Guard facilities throughout the state

complaint_resources.name: Delaware Office of the State Bank Commissioner
complaint_resources.description: Licenses and regulates lenders, mortgage companies, and credit services organizations. Handles complaints regarding payday loans, title loans, installment loans, and other consumer lending practices.

complaint_resources.name: Delaware Attorney General — Consumer Protection Unit
complaint_resources.description: Enforces the Delaware Consumer Fraud Act and other consumer protection laws. Investigates deceptive lending practices and provides consumer education resources.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about payday loans, title loans, personal loans, mortgages, and other consumer financial products. Provides federal-level enforcement for fair lending and truth in lending violations.

complaint_resources.name: Better Business Bureau — Delaware
complaint_resources.description: Provides consumer complaint mediation and business accreditation information for lenders and credit service providers.

statute_links.name: 5 Del. C. Chapter 22 — Delaware Consumer Credit Code
statute_links.description: Comprehensive regulation of consumer lending including personal loans, installment loans, and credit service organizations; includes usury provisions for unlicensed lenders

statute_links.name: 5 Del. C. § 2201 et seq. — Delaware Licensed Lenders Act
statute_links.description: Licensing and regulatory framework for payday lenders, title lenders, and other licensed money lenders; eliminates usury caps for licensed entities

statute_links.name: 5 Del. C. § 2227 — Usury Provisions for Licensed Lenders
statute_links.description: Statutory exemption from usury caps for licensed lenders under the Delaware Licensed Lenders Act

statute_links.name: 6 Del. C. § 4601-4620 — Delaware Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Regulation of credit repair companies including contract requirements, fee restrictions, and consumer cancellation rights

statute_links.name: 5 Del. C. § 2901 et seq. — Delaware Residential Mortgage Licensing Act
statute_links.description: Licensing and regulation of residential mortgage lenders and mortgage loan originators

statute_links.name: Delaware Consumer Fraud Act (6 Del. C. § 2511 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Prohibition of deceptive trade practices in lending and consumer transactions; provides enforcement authority to Attorney General

statute_links.name: Delaware Foreclosure Law (10 Del. C. § 1501 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Procedures for judicial foreclosure of mortgages in Delaware; requires court action and notice to borrower

DE.recent_changes: No significant changes to Delaware consumer lending or credit repair legislation reported for 2024-2025. Delaware maintains its historically lender-friendly regulatory environment with no usury caps for licensed lenders.

FL.name: Florida
FL.usury_cap: 18% for loans under $500,000; 25% criminal usury threshold; payday loans regulated separately
FL.consumer_protection_agency: Florida Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
key_regulations: Payday loans (deferred presentment) capped at $500 with maximum fee of $10 per $100 ($300) or $15 per $100 ($300-$500).
Borrowers can have only one outstanding payday loan at a time, tracked via a statewide database.
A mandatory 24-hour cooling-off period is required between payday loans.
The Florida Consumer Finance Act regulates installment lenders with tiered rate caps.
FL.consumer_rights_summary: Florida allows payday lending with notable consumer protections including a statewide database preventing multiple simultaneous loans, a $500 cap, and a 24-hour cooling-off period. The Office of Financial Regulation oversees all consumer lenders. Consumers can file complaints online through the OFR or the Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide clients with a written contract before any services are performed, clearly disclosing all terms, conditions, and the client's right to cancel
All contracts must include a statement that the client has the right to cancel within 3 business days without obligation
Credit repair companies are prohibited from charging or collecting any fees before services are delivered and the client's situation has demonstrably improved
Organizations cannot misrepresent their services, promise specific credit score increases, or claim ability to remove accurate negative information from credit reports
Written notice of the client's right to review their own credit report for free must be provided
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Florida Department of State, Division of Corporations

personal_loans.rate_cap: 18% APR for loans under $500,000; no cap for loans $500,000 and above
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans are regulated under Florida's usury laws (Fla. Stat. § 687.02). The Office of Financial Regulation oversees licensed lenders.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Maximum fee of $10 per $100 borrowed (minimum $10-$300 range) or $15 per $100 borrowed ($300-$500 range)
payday_loans.notes: Regulated under Fla. Stat. § 560.403. Borrowers tracked via the statewide deferred presentment database to prevent multiple simultaneous loans. A mandatory 24-hour cooling-off period is required between payday loans.

title_loans.notes: Title loans are prohibited in Florida.

installment_loans.rate_cap: Tiered rate caps under the Florida Consumer Finance Act (Fla. Stat. § 687.101-687.308): rates vary based on loan amount and structure; maximum rates generally range from 18-25% depending on loan size and repayment terms
installment_loans.notes: Installment lenders must be licensed by the Office of Financial Regulation. The Consumer Finance Act establishes specific rate schedules for different loan amounts.

mortgage.notes: Mortgages are regulated under Fla. Stat. §§ 655.059 (Mortgage Brokers), 655.001-655.059 (licensing), and foreclosure procedures are governed by Florida's judicial foreclosure process (Fla. Stat. § 702.01 et seq.). Florida requires judicial foreclosure for mortgages on residential properties. Mortgage brokers and lenders must be licensed by the Office of Financial Regulation.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) applies to all service members and their dependents in Florida, capping the Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) at 36% for covered borrowing (including payday loans, vehicle title loans, and installment loans). This federal cap preempts Florida's state usury laws for military borrowers.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) provides service members with protections including a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, protection from foreclosure and eviction during active duty, and the right to terminate certain contracts (including residential leases). Service members can request SCRA relief from creditors or courts.
state_specific_protections: Florida offers property tax exemptions for veterans with service-connected disabilities (Fla. Stat. § 196.001), which may indirectly support financial stability
Florida has the Florida Veterans' Foundation and the Office of the Governor's Veterans Advocate to assist military members with benefits and financial services complaints
Certain Florida state-chartered banks and credit unions offer specialized military lending and deposit products at favorable rates
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Florida has a robust VA loan market with state-approved VA loan servicers. The Florida Housing Finance Corporation administers veterans' homeownership programs. Florida does not impose state-specific VA loan regulations beyond federal requirements (38 U.S.C. § 3701 et seq.). Veterans may also be eligible for Florida's Homestead Property Tax Exemption (up to $50,000 in assessed value exemption).
veteran_protections.military_installations: Major military installations in Florida include Naval Station Mayport (Jacksonville), MacDill Air Force Base (Tampa), Naval Air Station Pensacola, Eglin Air Force Base, and Homestead Air Reserve Base. These installations are key enforcement areas for Military Lending Act compliance.

complaint_resources.name: Florida Office of Financial Regulation (OFR)
complaint_resources.description: Regulates consumer lenders, mortgage brokers, and credit service organizations. Accepts complaints about lenders, payday loan providers, and credit repair companies.

complaint_resources.name: Florida Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Handles consumer complaints, including credit repair fraud, predatory lending, and debt collection violations. Online complaint filing available.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal agency accepting complaints about credit cards, mortgages, payday loans, student loans, and other consumer financial products.

complaint_resources.name: Florida Department of State Division of Corporations
complaint_resources.description: Oversees registration and licensing of credit repair organizations operating in Florida.

complaint_resources.name: Better Business Bureau (BBB) - Florida
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints and provides ratings on consumer finance companies, credit repair services, and lenders.

statute_links.name: Florida Usury Law
statute_links.description: Establishes the 18% usury cap for loans under $500,000 and 25% criminal usury threshold.

statute_links.name: Florida Deferred Presentment Law (Payday Loans)
statute_links.description: Regulates payday loans, sets the $500 cap, establishes fee schedules, tracking database, and 24-hour cooling-off period.

statute_links.name: Florida Consumer Finance Act
statute_links.description: Governs installment lenders and sets tiered rate caps based on loan amounts.

statute_links.name: Florida Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair companies, prohibits upfront fees, requires 3-day cancellation rights, and mandates written contracts.

statute_links.name: Florida Mortgage Broker Licensing Law
statute_links.description: Establishes licensing requirements and regulations for mortgage brokers and mortgage lenders in Florida.

statute_links.name: Florida Foreclosure Procedures
statute_links.description: Establishes judicial foreclosure procedures for residential mortgages in Florida.

statute_links.name: Military Lending Act (Federal)
statute_links.description: Federal law capping MAPR at 36% for covered borrowing by service members, applies in Florida.

statute_links.name: Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (Federal)
