This applies to payday loans, vehicle title loans, and installment loans. Maine's stricter usury laws may provide additional protections in many cases.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3901 et seq.) caps interest on pre-service debts at 6%, provides foreclosure and eviction protections, allows lease termination rights, and mandates notice requirements. These protections apply to active duty members in Maine.
state_specific_protections: Maine does not have separate state-level military lending protections beyond federal MLA and SCRA, but the state's general 30% usury cap on supervised loans provides broad protection comparable to MLA
Maine Bureau of Consumer Credit Protection enforces compliance with federal military lending protections alongside state consumer credit laws
Servicemembers may file complaints with both state and federal regulators (CFPB, DoD) regarding violations of military lending protections
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Maine offers property tax exemption for disabled veterans (Me. Rev. Stat. Ann. tit. 36, § 652). VA loans are available through Maine's housing finance authority. No state-specific guarantee or subsidy program beyond federal VA loan benefits.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Naval Submarine Base New London (Connecticut, nearby); active duty military presence through National Guard and Reserve units. Brunswick Naval Air Station closed in 2011.

complaint_resources.name: Maine Bureau of Consumer Credit Protection
complaint_resources.description: Primary regulator for consumer lending, credit services organizations, and payday loan enforcement. Handles licensing, examinations, and consumer complaints regarding credit repair, lending practices, and usury violations.

complaint_resources.name: Maine Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Enforces consumer protection laws including unfair/deceptive practices, credit repair fraud, and lending violations. Accepts complaints and investigates violations of Maine consumer protection statutes.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal consumer complaints regarding payday loans, installment loans, credit repair services, mortgages, and other consumer financial products. Covers violations of federal law including TILA, FCRA, and ECOA.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints regarding credit repair fraud, identity theft, payday loan fraud, and other deceptive lending practices affecting Maine consumers.

statute_links.name: Maine Consumer Credit Code (tit. 9-A)
statute_links.description: Comprehensive statute governing personal loans, installment loans, residential mortgages, and other consumer credit transactions in Maine including rate caps and licensing requirements.

statute_links.name: Maine Consumer Credit Code — Loan Broker Provisions (tit. 9-A, Art. 10)
statute_links.description: Maine regulates credit repair organizations as loan brokers under the Consumer Credit Code, Title 9-A, Article 10 (§ 10-101 through 10-401). Includes licensing, cancellation rights, and fee restrictions.

statute_links.name: Maine Usury Laws (tit. 9-A, § 2-101 to § 2-103)
statute_links.description: Establishes the 30% APR cap for supervised loans under $2,000 and general usury restrictions on consumer credit transactions.

statute_links.name: Maine Residential Mortgage Lending Law (tit. 9-A, § 8-101 to § 8-402)
statute_links.description: Governs residential mortgage loans including licensing, high-cost mortgage restrictions, escrow requirements, and anti-prepayment penalty rules.

statute_links.name: Maine Foreclosure Law (tit. 14, § 6101 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Establishes Maine's judicial foreclosure process and borrower protections including right to cure and redemption periods.

ME.recent_changes: No significant changes to Maine consumer lending or credit repair laws in 2024-2025. Maine continues to enforce the existing 30% usury cap and comprehensive Consumer Credit Code. The state maintains its position of not having specific payday loan legislation, relying instead on general usury and licensing restrictions.

MD.name: Maryland
MD.usury_cap: 24% for consumer loans under $6,000 (33% for under $1,000); payday lending banned
MD.consumer_protection_agency: Maryland Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
MD.licensing_board: Maryland Office of the Commissioner of Financial Regulation
key_regulations: Payday lending is effectively banned through interest rate caps that make the business model unviable.
Consumer loans under $6,000 capped at 2% per month (24% APR); under $1,000 at 2.75% per month.
The Maryland Consumer Protection Act provides strong anti-predatory lending protections.
Licensed consumer finance companies must comply with strict rate and disclosure requirements.
MD.consumer_rights_summary: Maryland effectively bans payday lending through strict interest rate caps that make the business model impractical. The state has strong consumer protection laws including the Maryland Consumer Protection Act. Consumers can file complaints with the Commissioner of Financial Regulation or the Attorney General.

credit_repair_laws.statute_code: Md. Code Ann., Com. Law § 14-1901 to 14-1915
key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide written contract clearly stating all terms, conditions, and cancellation rights before requesting payment
Upfront payment for credit repair services is prohibited; fees can only be charged after services are completed
Customers have unconditional right to cancel contract within 5 business days without penalty or obligation
Credit repair organizations must not make false or misleading statements about their ability to improve credit or remove accurate negative information
Organizations cannot charge for monitoring or advising consumers to dispute accurate information
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Maryland Office of the Commissioner of Financial Regulation

personal_loans.rate_cap: 24% APR (2% per month) for loans under $6,000; 33% APR (2.75% per month) for loans under $1,000
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans from licensed consumer finance companies are regulated under Maryland's consumer loan rate caps. Loans must comply with disclosure requirements under the Maryland Consumer Loan Law.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Effectively banned through interest rate caps that make the business model unviable
payday_loans.notes: Maryland has effectively banned payday lending by imposing strict interest rate caps (24% APR for loans under $6,000) that make the payday loan business model financially impractical. No specific payday loan product is permitted.

title_loans.rate_cap: Effectively prohibited under consumer lending rate caps
title_loans.notes: Title loans are not permitted in Maryland. The state's strict interest rate caps and consumer protection laws effectively prohibit this lending type.

installment_loans.rate_cap: Subject to same rate caps as personal loans: 24% APR for loans under $6,000; 33% APR for loans under $1,000. Loans over $6,000 are not subject to statutory rate caps but must comply with disclosure requirements.
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are regulated under Md. Code Ann., Com. § 12-301 et seq. (Consumer Loan Law) and must include clear payment schedules, terms, and conditions. Licensed consumer finance companies must comply with all disclosure and documentation requirements.

mortgage.notes: Maryland mortgages are subject to both state and federal regulations. Mortgage lenders must be licensed with the Office of the Commissioner of Financial Regulation. Foreclosures in Maryland are judicial (non-judicial foreclosure not permitted), providing additional borrower protections. Borrowers have the right to cure defaults and redeem property in certain circumstances. The Maryland Residential Mortgage Loan Originator Act (Md. Code Ann., Com. § 11-601 et seq.) governs mortgage licensing and practices. High-cost mortgage (subprime) loans have additional restrictions and disclosures.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) applies to all active duty service members and their dependents in Maryland. The 36% MAPR (Military Annual Percentage Rate) cap includes interest, fees, charges, and credit insurance. Lenders must provide required disclosures and notice of the cap applies. The Department of Defense enforces compliance and maintains a Military Lending Act Compliance Database.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) applies to Maryland service members. SCRA limits pre-service debts to 6% interest, provides foreclosure and eviction protections, allows lease termination without penalty, and protects against default judgments. Service members must provide military status documentation (LES statement or military ID) to claim protections. Creditors cannot charge higher rates for SCRA-protected debts.
state_specific_protections: Maryland recognizes and enforces federal SCRA and MLA protections through state courts; service members can raise SCRA defenses in any state court proceeding
The Maryland Department of Veterans Affairs (MDVA) provides counseling and assistance to veterans regarding financial rights and predatory lending complaints
Maryland property tax credits are available to qualifying disabled veterans, providing financial relief on property tax obligations
Fort Meade (NSA) and Aberdeen Proving Ground are major military installations in Maryland; MLA enforcement efforts are focused in surrounding communities
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Maryland participates in VA home loan programs administered through the federal VA. The state does not have a separate state VA loan program but recognizes VA loans as part of mortgage underwriting. Veterans may also benefit from Maryland's homeownership assistance programs offered through the Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development, though these are not exclusively for VA loan holders.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort Meade (National Security Agency), Aberdeen Proving Ground (U.S. Army), Joint Base Andrews (U.S. Air Force), Naval Station Norfolk (nearby Virginia border)

complaint_resources.name: Maryland Office of the Commissioner of Financial Regulation
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints regarding consumer loans, credit repair organizations, mortgage lenders, and other financial service providers. Oversees licensing and enforcement of consumer finance laws.

complaint_resources.name: Maryland Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Enforces the Maryland Consumer Protection Act and investigates complaints about unfair, deceptive, or predatory lending practices. Accepts complaints online and by mail.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal agency that accepts complaints about consumer financial products and services, including lending and credit reporting issues. Complaints are reviewed and forwarded to relevant companies and regulators.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about fraud, deceptive practices, and identity theft related to consumer finance. Works with law enforcement and state attorneys general.

complaint_resources.name: Maryland Department of Veterans Affairs
complaint_resources.description: Provides resources and assistance to veterans regarding financial rights, benefits, and predatory lending complaints. Offers counseling and advocacy services.

statute_links.name: Maryland Consumer Loan Law (Md. Code Ann., Com. § 12-301 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Establishes interest rate caps, licensing requirements, and disclosure obligations for consumer loans under $6,000

statute_links.name: Maryland Credit Services Businesses Act (Md. Code Ann., Com. Law § 14-1901 to 14-1915)
statute_links.description: Regulates credit services businesses, including bonding requirements, contract terms, cancellation rights, and prohibition on upfront fees

statute_links.name: Maryland Consumer Protection Act (Md. Code Ann., Com. § 13-101 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Provides broad consumer protections against unfair, deceptive, and predatory practices in consumer transactions

statute_links.name: Maryland Residential Mortgage Loan Originator Act (Md. Code Ann., Com. § 11-601 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Governs licensing, conduct, and disclosure requirements for mortgage lenders and loan originators in Maryland

statute_links.name: Maryland Finance Company Law (Md. Code Ann., Com. § 12-401 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Regulates licensed consumer finance companies, including rate caps for loans under $6,000 and licensing requirements

statute_links.name: Maryland Judicial Foreclosure Law (Md. Code Ann., Real Prop. § 7-105)
statute_links.description: Establishes judicial foreclosure procedures for Maryland real property and borrower rights in foreclosure proceedings

MD.recent_changes: Maryland has maintained its strong consumer lending protections through consistently enforced rate caps and payday lending prohibition. No significant legislative changes affecting consumer lending or credit repair laws occurred in 2024-2025, though the Office of the Commissioner of Financial Regulation continues enforcement actions against predatory lenders and unlicensed finance companies attempting to circumvent state caps.

MA.name: Massachusetts
MA.usury_cap: 20% for consumer loans (criminal usury at 20%); payday lending banned
MA.consumer_protection_agency: Massachusetts Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
key_regulations: Payday lending is banned; the small loan statute cap of 23% APR makes it unviable.
Criminal usury threshold is 20% for most consumer loans.
The Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act (Chapter 93A) is among the strongest in the nation.
All consumer lenders must be licensed by the Division of Banks.
MA.consumer_rights_summary: Massachusetts bans payday lending through rate caps and has one of the nation's strongest consumer protection laws (Chapter 93A). The Division of Banks regulates all consumer lenders with strict requirements. Consumers can file complaints with the Division of Banks or the Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit repair companies must provide a written contract before charging any fees, detailing all services, timeline, and total cost
Clients have a three-business-day cancellation right to void the contract without penalty or obligation
Upfront fees are prohibited; payment must be collected only after services are fully performed
Companies cannot make false or misleading claims about ability to remove accurate negative information from credit reports
Companies must maintain a surety bond of at least $10,000 and register with the Massachusetts Attorney General
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Massachusetts Attorney General Consumer Protection Division

personal_loans.rate_cap: 20% APR (criminal usury threshold); small loan statute allows up to 23% APR but most lenders stay below 20% threshold
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans are legal and regulated by the Massachusetts Division of Banks. All consumer lenders must be licensed. Rate caps are enforced to prevent usurious lending.

payday_loans.rate_cap: De facto banned; 20% criminal usury cap and 23% small loan statute APR make payday lending economically unviable
payday_loans.notes: Payday lending is effectively banned in Massachusetts through strict rate caps. The 20% criminal usury threshold for consumer loans makes short-term, high-fee lending illegal. No licensed payday lenders operate in the state.

title_loans.rate_cap: Subject to 20% criminal usury cap; title lending is not explicitly authorized and effectively prohibited
title_loans.notes: Title loans are not legal in Massachusetts. The state's usury laws and consumer protection framework do not permit vehicle title-secured lending.

installment_loans.rate_cap: Subject to 20% APR criminal usury threshold and licensing requirements; rates vary by lender type and loan amount
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are legal in Massachusetts and regulated by the Division of Banks. Lenders must be licensed and comply with usury limits and Chapter 93A consumer protections.

mortgage.notes: Massachusetts mortgages are governed by Mass. Gen. Laws c. 244 (mortgages and deeds of trust). All mortgage lenders must be licensed by the Division of Banks. Foreclosures in Massachusetts are conducted through judicial process (judicial foreclosure required). Massachusetts has strong foreclosure protections including right to cure, notice requirements, and mediation opportunities. The state imposes restrictions on prepayment penalties and requires clear disclosure of loan terms. Reverse mortgages are regulated under federal HECM standards and state licensing requirements.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The federal Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) caps the Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) at 36% for covered active-duty service members and dependents. The MAPR includes interest, fees, and certain charges. The MLA applies to payday loans, title loans, installment loans, and other consumer credit in Massachusetts and is enforced by the CFPB.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) provides active-duty service members a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, including credit card debt, personal loans, and auto loans. SCRA also provides foreclosure protections (30-day notice and court approval required) and lease termination rights. These protections apply to Massachusetts residents on active duty.
state_specific_protections: Massachusetts recognizes the federal SCRA and MLA protections; state usury laws (20% cap) are stricter than federal MLA limits, providing additional protection for service members

The Massachusetts Division of Banks enforces licensing requirements for all consumer lenders, including those serving military populations, with oversight of compliance with federal military lending protections
Massachusetts allows service members and their families to access state-based financial assistance programs and counseling through the Massachusetts Office of Veteran Services
The state provides a Property Tax Exemption for surviving spouses and dependents of service members who died on active duty or from service-connected disability
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Massachusetts residents can utilize VA home loans backed by the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs. The state does not impose additional state-specific licensing or regulation on VA-backed loans beyond federal requirements. The Massachusetts Housing Finance Agency (MassHousing) offers down payment assistance and lending programs for veterans. The state Property Tax Exemption for veterans applies to those with service-connected disabilities rated 10% or higher by the VA.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Massachusetts hosts several military installations including Naval Station Norfolk operations, Coast Guard Station Cape Cod, Massachusetts Military Academy (Army National Guard), and various Air Force Reserve and National Guard facilities. Boston area installations serve a significant veteran and active-duty population.

complaint_resources.name: Massachusetts Division of Banks
complaint_resources.description: Primary regulator for consumer lenders, credit repair companies, and mortgage lenders in Massachusetts. Handles complaints about unlicensed lending, usurious rates, predatory practices, and violation of state lending laws.

complaint_resources.name: Massachusetts Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Enforces Chapter 93A (Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act), handles complaints about fraud, deceptive practices, credit repair scams, and general consumer financial abuse. Provides strong private right of action for consumers.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal regulator handling complaints about consumer financial products and services, including payday loans, title loans, mortgage servicing, debt collection, credit reporting, and Military Lending Act violations.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints about identity theft, credit repair fraud, and deceptive lending practices affecting Massachusetts consumers.

complaint_resources.name: National Foundation for Credit Counseling (NFCC)
complaint_resources.description: Provides free or low-cost credit counseling and financial education to Massachusetts residents; HUD-approved counseling agency.

statute_links.name: Massachusetts Usury Law (Criminal Usury)
statute_links.description: Mass. Gen. Laws c. 271, § 49 defines criminal usury at 20% APR for most consumer loans; foundational to Massachusetts lending restrictions.

statute_links.name: Massachusetts Small Loan Statute
statute_links.description: Mass. Gen. Laws c. 140, § 96 et seq. governs small loans, permits rates up to 23% APR, and establishes licensing requirements for small loan lenders.

statute_links.name: Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act (Chapter 93A)
statute_links.description: Mass. Gen. Laws c. 93A provides sweeping consumer protections against unfair/deceptive practices, private right of action, and double/treble damages; applies to all consumer lending and credit services.

statute_links.name: Massachusetts Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Mass. Gen. Laws c. 93, § 68 regulates credit repair companies, requires bonding, registration, written contracts, and prohibits upfront fees.

statute_links.name: Massachusetts Division of Banks Licensing Requirements
statute_links.description: Official guidance on licensing requirements for consumer lenders, mortgage lenders, and credit services companies operating in Massachusetts.

statute_links.name: Massachusetts Mortgage Foreclosure Law
statute_links.description: Mass. Gen. Laws c. 244 governs mortgage foreclosure procedures; Massachusetts requires judicial foreclosure with court approval and homeowner protections.

statute_links.name: Massachusetts Debt Collection Practices Act
statute_links.description: Mass. Gen. Laws c. 93, § 49 et seq. restricts abusive debt collection practices and provides consumer protections aligned with federal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act.

MA.recent_changes: No significant changes to core consumer lending or credit repair legislation in 2024-2025. Massachusetts maintains its strong existing regulatory framework with the 20% criminal usury cap, payday lending ban, and Chapter 93A consumer protections. The Division of Banks continues active enforcement of licensing and lending law compliance.

MI.name: Michigan
MI.usury_cap: 25% for consumer loans; payday loans capped at $600 with 15% fee on first $100, tiered after
MI.consumer_protection_agency: Michigan Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
MI.licensing_board: Michigan Department of Insurance and Financial Services
key_regulations: Payday loans (deferred presentment) capped at $600 with tiered fees: 15% first $100, 14% on $100-$200, 13% on $200-$300, etc.
Maximum loan term is 31 days; rollovers prohibited.
Borrowers limited to one payday loan at a time.
The Michigan Consumer Protection Act provides additional safeguards against deceptive lending.
MI.consumer_rights_summary: Michigan allows payday lending with a $600 cap, tiered fee structure, and a one-loan-at-a-time limit. Rollovers are prohibited. The Department of Insurance and Financial Services regulates consumer lenders, and complaints can be filed with DIFS or the Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit repair companies must provide a written contract clearly stating services, timeline, costs, and cancellation rights before any work begins
All written communications with credit reporting agencies must be disclosed to the consumer; companies cannot guarantee removal of accurate information
Upfront fees are prohibited; payment must be contingent upon actual results and services performed
Consumers have an unconditional right to cancel within 3 business days without penalty or obligation
Companies must maintain a surety bond and comply with the Michigan Consumer Protection Act's prohibition on unfair, unconscionable, and deceptive methods
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Michigan Department of Insurance and Financial Services (DIFS)

personal_loans.rate_cap: 25% APR for consumer loans (MCL 445.851 et seq.)
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans by licensed consumer lenders are capped at 25% APR under Michigan's consumer loan law. Unlicensed lenders may be subject to different rules. The Department of Insurance and Financial Services licenses and regulates consumer lenders.

payday_loans.notes: Payday loans (deferred presentment) are legal and capped at $600. Maximum loan term is 31 days. Borrowers are limited to one payday loan at a time. Rollovers are prohibited. The tiered fee structure is regulated under MCL 487.2152. Lenders must be licensed by DIFS.

title_loans.notes: Title loans are not explicitly legalized in Michigan and are generally not permitted as a distinct lending product. Vehicles as collateral may be subject to other security interest laws.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 25% APR cap applies to consumer installment loans under MCL 445.851 et seq.
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans made by licensed consumer lenders are subject to the 25% APR usury cap. Terms, payment schedules, and financing charges must comply with the Michigan Consumer Loan Act and rules promulgated by DIFS.

mortgage.notes: Michigan mortgages are subject to state and federal regulations. Foreclosure in Michigan is non-judicial through power of sale in the mortgage document, though judicial foreclosure is also available. The Michigan Residential Mortgage Act (MCL 445.1651 et seq.) regulates residential mortgage lenders and brokers. DIFS licenses mortgage lenders and servicers. Michigan requires a 120-day notice and opportunity to cure before foreclosure sale. Homeowners have a redemption period after foreclosure sale.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The federal Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) caps the annual percentage rate at 36% for covered borrowers (active duty service members and their dependents). This rate cap includes all interest, fees, and charges. The MLA applies in Michigan to all consumer credit products covered by the law, regardless of state law rate caps.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) provides covered service members with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts (consumer credit incurred before military service), protection against foreclosure and eviction during military service with proper notice, ability to terminate certain contracts and leases, and suspension of civil proceedings. Michigan courts enforce SCRA protections.
state_specific_protections: Michigan's Consumer Protection Act (MCL 445.903) applies to all lenders and provides safeguards against deceptive and unfair practices affecting service members and their families
Military service members and spouses may be eligible for property tax exemptions on homestead properties under MCL 211.7cc (disability exemption applies to disabled veterans)
Michigan does not impose additional state-specific rate caps beyond federal MLA requirements for covered military borrowers; the 25% consumer loan cap applies to non-covered military consumers
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Michigan has no state-specific VA loan program but recognizes federal VA loan benefits. The Michigan Housing Development Authority may offer complementary programs. Property tax exemptions are available for disabled veterans (homestead property tax exemption under MCL 211.7cc). Veterans may also qualify for veteran-specific mortgage programs through federal VA Loans.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort Custer Training Center (Battle Creek), Camp Grayling (Grayling), Air National Guard installations in several locations. The Great Lakes Naval Station is located in nearby Illinois but services Michigan residents.

complaint_resources.name: Michigan Department of Insurance and Financial Services (DIFS)
complaint_resources.description: Regulates consumer lenders, payday lenders, mortgage lenders, and credit repair companies. Handles complaints about unlicensed lending, rate violations, and regulatory violations. Submit complaints through their website.

complaint_resources.name: Michigan Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Investigates and prosecutes deceptive lending practices, fraud, and violations of the Michigan Consumer Protection Act. Accepts complaints related to payday loans, predatory lending, credit repair scams, and other consumer finance issues.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal agency that accepts complaints about consumer financial products and services, including payday loans, personal loans, mortgages, credit repair services, and debt collection. Complaints are reviewed and forwarded to lenders for response.

complaint_resources.name: Better Business Bureau - Michigan
complaint_resources.description: Collects and mediates complaints about consumer finance companies and credit services. Provides accreditation ratings and complaint resolution services.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about credit repair companies, identity theft, predatory lending, debt collection, and other deceptive or unfair consumer financial practices. Coordinates with state authorities.

statute_links.name: Michigan Consumer Loan Act
statute_links.description: Establishes the 25% APR usury cap for consumer loans, licensing requirements, and consumer protections for personal installment loans.

statute_links.name: Michigan Payday Loan Regulations (Deferred Presentment Services)
statute_links.description: Governs payday loan products, including the $600 cap, tiered fee structure, 31-day maximum term, one-loan-at-a-time restriction, and rollover prohibition.

statute_links.name: Michigan Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair companies, requires written contracts, prohibits upfront fees, mandates surety bonds, and provides consumers with a 3-day cancellation right.

statute_links.name: Michigan Consumer Protection Act
statute_links.description: Broadly prohibits unfair, unconscionable, and deceptive methods, acts, and practices in consumer transactions, including lending and credit services.

statute_links.name: Michigan Residential Mortgage Act
statute_links.description: Regulates residential mortgage lenders, brokers, and servicers, establishes licensing and conduct standards, and provides consumer protections in mortgage transactions.

statute_links.name: Michigan Foreclosure Procedures and Redemption Rights
statute_links.description: Governs non-judicial foreclosure through power of sale, notice requirements (120 days), and post-foreclosure redemption rights for homeowners.

statute_links.name: Michigan Homestead Property Tax Exemption for Veterans
statute_links.description: Provides property tax exemptions for disabled veterans on homestead properties.

MI.recent_changes: No significant changes to Michigan's payday loan cap, consumer loan usury rate, or credit repair laws in 2024-2025. Michigan's regulatory framework for consumer lending remains relatively stable, though DIFS continues to enforce existing rules against unlicensed lending and deceptive practices. Proposed legislative efforts have focused on strengthening enforcement rather than substantive rate or fee modifications.

MN.name: Minnesota
MN.usury_cap: 8% default; payday loans capped at $350 with tiered fees
MN.consumer_protection_agency: Minnesota Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
key_regulations: Payday loans capped at $350 with tiered fees ($5.50 per $100 up to $50, $10 per $100 on $50-$100, etc.).
Minimum loan term of 30 days required for payday loans.
Licensed consumer small loan lenders subject to tiered rate caps.
The Minnesota Consumer Fraud Act provides protections against predatory lending.
MN.consumer_rights_summary: Minnesota allows payday lending with a $350 cap, tiered fee structure, and a minimum 30-day term requirement. The Department of Commerce regulates all consumer lenders. Consumers benefit from the Minnesota Consumer Fraud Act and can file complaints with the Department of Commerce or Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit services organizations must provide written contracts before performing any services, clearly disclosing all terms, conditions, and charges
Prohibition on making false or misleading statements about credit repair or credit improvement services to consumers
Requirement to inform consumers of their right to obtain credit reports and dispute inaccuracies directly with credit bureaus at no cost
Credit repair companies cannot charge upfront fees; all compensation must be contingent on actual results
Prohibition on inducing consumers to waive rights under the Fair Credit Reporting Act or other consumer protection laws
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Minnesota Department of Commerce

personal_loans.rate_cap: 8% APR default usury cap; licensed consumer small loan lenders subject to tiered rate caps not to exceed 36% APR for loans under $1,000
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans regulated under Minn. Stat. § 334.01 et seq. Rates vary based on loan amount and lender license class. Unlicensed lenders subject to 8% usury cap.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Tiered fee structure: $5.50 per $100 borrowed for loans up to $50; $10 per $100 for loans $50.01-$100; $15 per $100 for loans over $100
payday_loans.notes: Payday loans capped at $350 maximum principal. Minimum loan term of 30 days required. Only one outstanding payday loan permitted at a time per consumer. Lenders must participate in the Statewide Payday Loan Database (Minn. Stat. § 56.125) to prevent predatory lending cycles. Licensed under Minn. Stat. § 56.01 et seq.

title_loans.notes: Title loans are not explicitly authorized in Minnesota. Vehicles securing loans may be subject to existing secured lending regulations, but title-secured lending as typically practiced is not a recognized lending category.

installment_loans.rate_cap: Licensed consumer small loan lenders: tiered rates capped at 36% APR for loans under $1,000; rates vary by license class. Unlicensed lenders subject to 8% default usury cap.
installment_loans.notes: Regulated under Minn. Stat. § 334.01 et seq. (Consumer Small Loan Lenders). Installment loans under $5,000 with terms of 2 years or less regulated by Department of Commerce. Lender must be licensed unless exempt.

mortgage.notes: Mortgages regulated under Minn. Stat. § 507.18 (foreclosure), Minn. Stat. § 580.01 et seq. (general mortgage provisions). Minnesota permits non-judicial foreclosure through power of sale if authorized in mortgage document. Judicial foreclosure also available. Residential mortgages subject to licensing requirements for mortgage originators (Minn. Stat. § 57.09) through the Department of Commerce. Right of redemption permitted in certain foreclosure contexts. State requires notice of default and opportunity to cure before acceleration.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 3953) caps the Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) at 36% for covered borrowers (active duty service members and their dependents). This applies to most consumer credit products in Minnesota and preempts state law when state caps are higher. The MLA also requires specific disclosures, prohibits certain debt-collection practices, and restricts mandatory arbitration clauses.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3951 et seq.) provides active duty service members with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, protection from default judgments and foreclosures without court appearance, lease termination rights, and restrictions on eviction. These protections apply in Minnesota for eligible active duty personnel.
state_specific_protections: Minnesota recognizes veterans' property tax exemptions under Minn. Stat. § 273.13, which may provide relief on mortgaged properties for disabled veterans, reducing financial burden of homeownership
State mortgage foreclosure notice requirements (Minn. Stat. § 580.03) apply to all foreclosures including those affecting service members, ensuring due process protections
Minnesota Department of Veterans Affairs provides counseling, benefits information, and referrals to financial assistance programs for veterans in financial distress
State law prohibits discrimination in lending based on military status, consistent with federal protections
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Minnesota does not offer a state-specific VA loan program, but federal VA loans (38 U.S.C. § 3703 et seq.) are widely available through private lenders in the state. Disabled veterans may qualify for property tax exemptions. The Minnesota Department of Veterans Affairs (part of the Minnesota Department of Employment and Economic Development) provides veteran-specific housing information and referrals.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort Ripley (Camp Ripley) near Little Falls; Naval Station Great Lakes (offshore); Minnesota has significant National Guard and Reserve presence. The Military Lending Act is primarily enforced by DOD, CFPB, and state regulators in areas with military population concentration.

complaint_resources.name: Minnesota Department of Commerce
complaint_resources.description: Regulates consumer lenders, payday lenders, credit repair organizations, and mortgage originators. Handles licensing, compliance, and complaints related to predatory lending and unfair practices.

complaint_resources.name: Minnesota Attorney General – Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Enforces the Minnesota Consumer Fraud Act and investigates complaints related to deceptive lending practices, credit repair fraud, and predatory lending. Accepts complaints online and by mail.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal agency handling complaints about consumer financial products and services, including loans, credit reporting, and debt collection practices. Can refer complaints to state regulators.

complaint_resources.name: Better Business Bureau – Minnesota
complaint_resources.description: Provides complaint resolution services and business rating information for lenders and credit services companies operating in Minnesota.

complaint_resources.name: Legal Aid Minnesota
complaint_resources.description: Provides free legal assistance to low-income Minnesotans facing lending disputes, foreclosure defense, and predatory lending claims.

statute_links.name: Minnesota Payday Loan Regulations (Minn. Stat. § 56.01-56.18)
statute_links.description: Comprehensive regulation of payday lending, including $350 cap, tiered fees, 30-day minimum term, and Statewide Payday Loan Database requirements

statute_links.name: Minnesota Consumer Small Loan Lender Act (Minn. Stat. § 334.01-334.27)
statute_links.description: Governs licensed consumer small loan lenders, rate caps, and licensing requirements for non-payday installment and personal loans

statute_links.name: Minnesota Credit Services Organization Act (Minn. Stat. § 332.52-332.60)
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair companies, prohibits upfront fees, requires bonding and registration, provides consumer cancellation rights

statute_links.name: Minnesota Consumer Fraud Act (Minn. Stat. § 325D.43-325D.66)
statute_links.description: Provides broad consumer protections against deceptive and unfair practices in commerce, including predatory lending

statute_links.name: Minnesota Mortgage Foreclosure Law (Minn. Stat. § 580.01-580.30)
statute_links.description: Establishes procedures for residential and non-residential mortgage foreclosure, notice requirements, and redemption rights

statute_links.name: Minnesota Mortgage Originator Licensing (Minn. Stat. § 57.09)
statute_links.description: Licensing and conduct requirements for mortgage loan originators and mortgage brokers in Minnesota

statute_links.name: Minnesota Usury Law (Minn. Stat. § 334.01)
statute_links.description: Establishes 8% default usury cap for unlicensed lenders and basic interest rate restrictions

statute_links.name: Minnesota Department of Commerce – Statewide Payday Loan Database
statute_links.description: Mandatory database for payday loan tracking to prevent consumers from holding multiple outstanding loans (Minn. Stat. § 56.125)

MN.recent_changes: No significant changes to Minnesota's core payday lending or credit repair statutes in 2024-2025. Ongoing regulatory focus by the Minnesota Department of Commerce and Attorney General on enforcement of existing protections, particularly regarding database compliance and unlicensed lending detection.

MS.name: Mississippi
MS.usury_cap: 10% contract rate (15% default); payday loans regulated under Check Cashers Act
MS.consumer_protection_agency: Mississippi Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
MS.licensing_board: Mississippi Department of Banking and Consumer Finance
key_regulations: Payday loans capped at $500 per lender with a maximum fee of $21.95 per $100.
Borrowers may have up to $500 from any single lender but there is no statewide cap on multiple lenders.
The Check Cashers Act governs payday lending with licensing requirements.
Mississippi has no centralized payday loan database.
MS.consumer_rights_summary: Mississippi allows payday lending with a $500 per-lender cap and $21.95 per $100 fee. The state has the lowest median income and highest poverty rate, making residents especially vulnerable to high-cost lending. Complaints can be filed with the Department of Banking and Consumer Finance or the Attorney General.

credit_repair_laws.state_statute: Mississippi Debt Management Services Act (no standalone CSO act — federal CROA applies)
key_provisions: Credit services organizations must provide written contract before any services rendered, with clear disclosure of all terms and conditions
Prohibited from charging or collecting fees until services are actually performed and results achieved
Must disclose the consumer's right to dispute inaccurate credit reporting information directly with credit bureaus at no cost
Cannot make false or misleading statements about services, results, or timeframes for credit improvement
Prohibited from advising consumers to dispute accurate information on credit reports
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Mississippi Department of Banking and Consumer Finance

personal_loans.rate_cap: 10% contract rate (15% default rate); subject to general usury law
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans governed by Mississippi usury statutes. Rate cap applies unless parties agree otherwise in writing for specific categories.

payday_loans.rate_cap: $21.95 per $100 borrowed (approximately 219.5% APR on 2-week loan)
payday_loans.notes: Governed by Mississippi Check Cashers Act (Miss. Code Ann. § 75-67-101 et seq.). Borrowers may obtain $500 from a single lender with no statewide cap on total outstanding loans from multiple lenders. No centralized database exists to track multiple loans. Lenders must be licensed by the Department of Banking and Consumer Finance.

title_loans.rate_cap: Not applicable — title loans are prohibited in Mississippi
title_loans.notes: Mississippi prohibits title lending under state law, though consumers may attempt to access title loans from lenders in other states.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 10% per annum (15% default); subject to Mississippi usury law (Miss. Code Ann. § 75-17-1 et seq.)
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are legal and regulated under general usury and consumer credit laws. Regulated installment lenders must comply with licensing requirements and disclosure obligations.

mortgage.notes: Mortgages in Mississippi are subject to the state's usury laws and foreclosure procedures. Mississippi permits non-judicial foreclosure (through deed of trust with power of sale) as well as judicial foreclosure. Residential mortgages are licensed through the Mississippi Department of Banking and Consumer Finance. The state follows primarily non-judicial foreclosure procedures for trust deeds.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) caps the Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) at 36% for covered active-duty service members and their dependents. This cap applies to all covered consumer credit products, including payday loans. The MAPR includes interest plus most fees. Lenders must provide clear written disclosures about the 36% cap.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3951 et seq.) limits interest on pre-service debts to 6% for active-duty service members. It provides protections against foreclosure, eviction, and vehicle repossession while on active duty or within 9 months thereafter. Lease termination rights are available for service members receiving military orders for permanent change of station.
state_specific_protections: Mississippi does not impose additional state-level interest rate caps below the federal MLA 36% threshold; federal protections are primary
The Mississippi Department of Banking and Consumer Finance enforces MLA compliance for licensed payday and installment lenders operating in the state
Service members and veterans may file complaints with the Mississippi Attorney General Consumer Protection Division if MLA violations occur
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Mississippi has no statewide VA loan subsidy program but benefits from the federal VA loan guarantee program administered through the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs. The state does not impose state-level property taxes on primary residences purchased with VA loans. Veterans may qualify for homestead property tax exemptions under Miss. Code Ann. § 27-33-1 et seq. if they meet state residency and disability requirements.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Major military installations include Naval Station Pascagoula (U.S. Navy), Keesler Air Force Base near Biloxi, Camp Shelby Joint Forces Training Center near Hattiesburg, and Naval Construction Battalion Center Gulfport. These installations house significant numbers of active-duty service members and families covered under the Military Lending Act.

complaint_resources.name: Mississippi Department of Banking and Consumer Finance
complaint_resources.description: Regulates payday lenders, check cashers, credit repair organizations, mortgage lenders, and other consumer finance entities. Handles complaints related to unlicensed lending, fee violations, and regulatory non-compliance.

complaint_resources.name: Mississippi Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Enforces consumer protection laws including payday lending regulations, credit repair laws, and fraud. Accepts complaints about deceptive lending practices and violations of the Mississippi Consumer Protection Act.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about payday loans, credit repair services, mortgages, and other consumer financial products. Federal oversight of lending practices and enforcement of the Military Lending Act.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints about credit repair fraud, identity theft, and predatory lending practices affecting Mississippi consumers.

statute_links.name: Mississippi Check Cashers Act
statute_links.description: Primary payday lending regulation statute (Miss. Code Ann. § 75-67-101 et seq.). Establishes licensing requirements, fee caps at $21.95 per $100, and $500 per-lender limits.

statute_links.name: Mississippi Usury Law
statute_links.description: General usury statute (Miss. Code Ann. § 75-17-1 et seq.) capping interest rates at 10% contract rate and 15% default rate for non-covered loans.

statute_links.name: Mississippi Debt Management Services Act
statute_links.description: Regulates debt management and credit services (Miss. Code Ann. § 81-22-1 et seq.). Mississippi has no standalone CSO act — federal CROA provides primary credit repair protections.

statute_links.name: Mississippi Consumer Protection Act
statute_links.description: Broad consumer protection statute (Miss. Code Ann. § 75-24-1 et seq.) prohibiting unfair and deceptive practices in consumer transactions, including lending.

statute_links.name: Mississippi Homestead Property Tax Exemption
statute_links.description: Provides property tax exemptions for qualifying veterans under Miss. Code Ann. § 27-33-1 et seq.

MS.recent_changes: No significant changes to Mississippi consumer lending or credit repair legislation in 2024-2025. The state's payday lending framework under the Check Cashers Act and credit repair regulations have remained stable since prior legislative sessions. Enforcement focus continues on unlicensed lending and Military Lending Act compliance.

MO.name: Missouri
MO.usury_cap: No cap for licensed lenders; payday loans capped at $500 with fees up to $75
MO.consumer_protection_agency: Missouri Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
key_regulations: Payday loans capped at $500 with maximum fee of 75% of the original loan amount.
Maximum loan term is 14-31 days.
Borrowers may renew up to 6 times, but principal must decrease by 5% with each renewal.
Licensed consumer lenders face no general interest rate cap.
MO.consumer_rights_summary: Missouri allows payday lending with relatively permissive regulations including up to 6 renewals. The fee cap of 75% of the loan amount results in very high effective APRs. The Division of Finance regulates consumer lenders, and complaints can be filed with the Division or the Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide written contract with detailed terms, services, and cost disclosures before any services rendered
Upfront fees are prohibited; payment only allowed after services are actually performed
Customers have unconditional right to cancel contract within 3 business days without penalty or obligation
Organizations must not misrepresent services or make false promises about credit repair outcomes
Cannot advise customers to dispute accurate information on credit reports
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Missouri Division of Finance

personal_loans.rate_cap: No specific cap for licensed lenders; regulated by Missouri Division of Finance
personal_loans.notes: Licensed consumer lenders in Missouri face no general interest rate cap. Personal loans are governed by general lending regulations enforced by the Division of Finance.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Maximum fee of 75% of original loan amount
payday_loans.notes: Payday loans capped at $500 maximum principal. Loan term must be 14-31 days. Borrowers may renew up to 6 times with mandatory 5% principal reduction per renewal. Fee cap of 75% results in effective APR of 391-652% depending on term. Regulated by Missouri Division of Finance.

title_loans.notes: Title loans are not explicitly authorized under Missouri law and are effectively prohibited.

installment_loans.rate_cap: No general cap for licensed lenders; governed by Mo. Rev. Stat. § 367.010-367.210 (Consumer Loan Law)
installment_loans.notes: Licensed installment lenders must comply with Truth in Lending Act (TILA) and other federal regulations. Missouri's Consumer Loan Law allows licensed lenders to charge reasonable rates without a statutory cap.

mortgage.notes: Mortgages in Missouri are regulated by state banking laws and federal regulations (TILA, RESPA, CFPB rules). Foreclosure is judicial; lenders must file suit in court. Redemption period applies post-sale. Missouri does not have a specific state mortgage licensing board separate from banking regulation. Non-judicial foreclosure is not permitted in Missouri.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The federal Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) establishes a 36% MAPR (Military Annual Percentage Rate) cap for active-duty service members on consumer credit products including payday loans, title loans, and installment loans. Missouri's payday loan fee structure (75% of principal) may exceed this 36% MAPR cap and would be superseded by federal MLA protections for eligible service members.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) provides 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, protects against eviction and foreclosure without court order, allows lease termination without penalty, and suspends statutes of limitations during active duty. These protections apply to all service members regardless of state.
state_specific_protections: Missouri recognizes Purple Heart recipients and disabled veterans for certain property tax exemptions, though not directly related to lending regulations
Missouri Veterans Commission administers benefits and services but does not have lending-specific protections beyond federal law
No state-specific predatory lending protections beyond federal MLA for active-duty service members
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Missouri recognizes VA-backed loans and complies with VA Loan Guaranty program requirements. No state-specific VA loan enhancements or separate state housing authority programs for VA loans. Veterans may benefit from Missouri's property tax exemptions in certain counties, but these do not affect lending terms.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort Leonard Wood (Pulaski County) - major Army installation; Whiteman Air Force Base (Johnson County); Scott Air Force Base (Illinois, near Missouri border); significant military population in Springfield and Kansas City metro areas

complaint_resources.name: Missouri Division of Finance
complaint_resources.description: Primary regulator for consumer lenders, payday lenders, and finance companies. Handles licensing, examination, and complaints regarding lending practices and consumer loan violations.

complaint_resources.name: Missouri Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Investigates consumer complaints regarding unfair/deceptive practices, credit repair fraud, payday lending violations, and other consumer finance violations. Files enforcement actions and provides consumer education.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal agency accepting complaints about payday loans, personal loans, credit reporting, debt collection, and other consumer financial products. Investigates patterns of violations and enforces federal consumer protection laws.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints about credit repair fraud, identity theft, unfair lending practices, and deceptive practices by non-bank lenders and credit service organizations.

complaint_resources.name: Missouri Better Business Bureau
complaint_resources.description: Maintains complaint records and ratings for lending and credit service companies operating in Missouri; provides consumer education and dispute resolution services.

statute_links.name: Missouri Payday Loan Law
statute_links.description: Mo. Rev. Stat. § 367.600-367.652 - Primary statute governing payday lending including loan caps, fee limits, renewal restrictions, and licensing requirements

statute_links.name: Missouri Consumer Loan Law
statute_links.description: Mo. Rev. Stat. § 367.010-367.210 - Governs installment loans and consumer credit transactions; establishes licensing and disclosure requirements

statute_links.name: Missouri Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Mo. Rev. Stat. § 407.635-407.644 - Regulates credit repair companies and credit counseling services; prohibits upfront fees and requires cancellation rights

statute_links.name: Missouri Division of Finance Licensing and Regulations
statute_links.description: Official licensing portal and regulatory guidance for consumer lenders, payday loan companies, and related financial service providers in Missouri

statute_links.name: Missouri Revised Statutes - Financial Institutions Code
statute_links.description: Chapter 367 contains comprehensive lending regulations including payday loans, consumer loans, and related consumer finance protections

statute_links.name: Missouri Attorney General Consumer Protection Statutes
statute_links.description: Chapter 407 includes credit services organization regulations, unfair/deceptive practices law, and consumer protection enforcement authority

MO.recent_changes: No significant changes to Missouri's core payday lending or credit repair laws in 2024-2025. Missouri continues to maintain relatively permissive payday lending regulations without recent restrictive amendments. Ongoing enforcement focus remains on compliance with existing fee caps ($75 or 75% of principal) and renewal limitations.

MT.name: Montana
MT.usury_cap: 36% APR cap on consumer loans (Initiative I-164, 2010); payday lending effectively banned
MT.consumer_protection_agency: Montana Attorney General Consumer Protection Office
MT.licensing_board: Montana Division of Banking and Financial Institutions
key_regulations: Ballot Initiative I-164 (2010) capped all consumer loans at 36% APR, effectively banning payday lending.
Licensed consumer lenders must comply with the Montana Consumer Loan Act.
The Montana Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act provides additional protections.
Title loans are also subject to the 36% APR cap.
MT.consumer_rights_summary: Montana voters approved a 36% APR cap in 2010, effectively banning payday lending in the state. All consumer loans, including title loans, are subject to this cap. Consumers can file complaints with the Division of Banking and Financial Institutions or the Attorney General.

credit_repair_laws.state_statute: No standalone state Credit Services Organization Act — federal CROA applies; Montana Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act (Mont. Code Ann. § 30-14-103) covers deceptive credit repair
credit_repair_laws.statute_code: Mont. Code Ann. § 30-14-103 (UTPA — deceptive practices including credit repair)
key_provisions: Credit services organizations must provide a written contract that clearly discloses all charges, services to be performed, and the timeframe for results
Prohibits collection of fees before services are actually performed; upfront fees are banned
Requires a three-day cancellation period during which consumers can void the contract without penalty
Prohibits false or misleading representations about credit repair capabilities or credit rights
Prohibits advising consumers to dispute accurate information on credit reports
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Montana Attorney General Consumer Protection Office (enforcement only — no state CSO registration exists)

personal_loans.rate_cap: 36% APR (Initiative I-164, 2010)
personal_loans.notes: All consumer loans, including personal loans, are subject to the 36% APR usury cap. Licensed consumer lenders must comply with the Montana Consumer Loan Act (Mont. Code Ann. § 32-27-101 et seq.). Default and prepayment penalties are permitted but must be reasonable and disclosed.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Effectively banned by 36% APR cap
payday_loans.notes: Initiative I-164 (2010) banned payday lending by capping all consumer loans at 36% APR. Payday lenders cannot legally operate in Montana. Lenders attempting to circumvent this law through other mechanisms also fall under the usury cap.

title_loans.notes: Title loans are subject to the same 36% APR usury cap as all other consumer loans. The cap makes traditional high-interest title lending illegal in Montana. Any title-secured loan must comply with the 36% APR ceiling.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 36% APR (Initiative I-164, 2010)
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are permitted under Montana law and governed by the Montana Consumer Loan Act (Mont. Code Ann. § 32-27-101 et seq.). All terms, including interest rate, fees, and repayment schedule, must comply with the 36% APR cap. Licensing is required for consumer lenders offering installment credit.

mortgage.notes: Montana mortgages are regulated under Mont. Code Ann. § 32-21-101 et seq. (Residential Mortgage Loan Act). Foreclosure is non-judicial, conducted through power of sale in the deed of trust or mortgage. Judicial foreclosure is also permitted. Notice requirements vary by type of instrument. Homeowner protections include right to cure/reinstate period and anti-deficiency limitations in certain circumstances. Mortgage lenders and brokers must be licensed with the Montana Division of Banking and Financial Institutions.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (10 U.S.C. § 987) establishes a 36% MAPR (Military Annual Percentage Rate) cap on covered loans to service members and their dependents. Montana's 36% APR state usury cap aligns with federal MLA protections, ensuring service members receive at least the federal standard. MAPR includes all fees and charges and applies to payday loans, vehicle title loans, and tax refund anticipation loans.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3101 et seq.) provides service members with protections including: 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts; protection against foreclosure, repossession, and eviction during active duty; lease termination rights; and requirement for court approval before default judgment. SCRA applies regardless of state law and supersedes less favorable state provisions.
state_specific_protections: Montana recognizes service-connected disability tax exemptions and property tax relief programs for disabled veterans through Mont. Code Ann. § 15-6-135
Montana provides a Homeowners Assistance Fund and veterans' loan program through the Montana Board of Housing, offering favorable lending terms to eligible veterans
Montana law recognizes military service in licensing and employment contexts, requiring state agencies to consider military training and experience for professional licensure
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Montana does not impose a state VA loan funding fee and honors VA loan guarantees fully. The Montana Board of Housing administers veterans' housing programs and works with the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs. Property tax assessments in Montana include exemptions for service-connected disabled veterans (Mont. Code Ann. § 15-6-135).
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort Harrison (near Helena) is a major VA medical center. Malmstrom Air Force Base (near Great Falls) is a major active-duty installation. Naval Submarine Base Kings Bay-adjacent recruiting and support operates throughout the state.

complaint_resources.name: Montana Division of Banking and Financial Institutions
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints against licensed lenders, credit services organizations, mortgage lenders, and other financial service providers. Enforces consumer lending laws, usury caps, and credit repair regulations.

complaint_resources.name: Montana Attorney General Consumer Protection Office
complaint_resources.description: Addresses unfair trade practices, consumer protection violations, fraud, and predatory lending under the Montana Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act (Mont. Code Ann. § 30-14-101 et seq.). Accepts complaints online and by mail.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal agency handling complaints about consumer financial products and services, including lending and credit reporting violations. Complaints are referred to relevant state and federal regulators.

complaint_resources.name: Better Business Bureau - Montana
complaint_resources.description: Local BBB chapters in Montana (Billings, Missoula, Great Falls) accept consumer complaints and provide business ratings and dispute resolution services.

complaint_resources.name: Montana Legal Services Association
complaint_resources.description: Provides free legal assistance to eligible low-income Montanans on consumer finance, lending disputes, and foreclosure issues.

statute_links.name: Initiative I-164 (2010) - Usury Cap
statute_links.description: Montana Constitution and implementing statutes establishing the 36% APR cap on all consumer loans, effectively banning payday and title lending

statute_links.name: Montana Consumer Loan Act
statute_links.description: Mont. Code Ann. § 32-27-101 et seq. Governs licensing, disclosures, and operations of consumer lenders offering personal loans, installment loans, and other consumer credit products

statute_links.name: Montana Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act (Credit Repair Provisions)
statute_links.description: Mont. Code Ann. § 30-14-103. Montana has no standalone CSO act. Credit repair companies are regulated under the UTPA deceptive practices provisions. Federal CROA provides primary protections.

statute_links.name: Montana Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act
statute_links.description: Mont. Code Ann. § 30-14-101 et seq. Provides broad consumer protections against unfair, unconscionable, and deceptive trade practices in lending and other consumer transactions

statute_links.name: Montana Residential Mortgage Loan Act
statute_links.description: Mont. Code Ann. § 32-21-101 et seq. Regulates mortgage lenders, brokers, and servicers; establishes licensing and disclosure requirements

statute_links.name: Montana Foreclosure and Non-Judicial Sale Procedures
statute_links.description: Mont. Code Ann. § 70-35-101 et seq. and § 70-34-101 et seq. Establishes procedures for non-judicial foreclosure through power of sale and judicial foreclosure actions

statute_links.name: Montana Division of Banking and Financial Institutions - Rules
statute_links.description: Administrative rules (ARM) implementing state lending and credit regulation, including consumer protection and licensing requirements

MT.recent_changes: No significant legislative changes to consumer lending or credit repair laws occurred in 2024-2025. Montana's 36% APR usury cap (Initiative I-164, 2010) remains the primary driver of consumer lending policy, with ongoing enforcement by the Division of Banking and Financial Institutions and Attorney General.

NE.name: Nebraska
NE.usury_cap: 36% APR cap on payday loans (Initiative 428, 2020); 16% general usury cap
NE.consumer_protection_agency: Nebraska Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
NE.licensing_board: Nebraska Department of Banking and Finance
key_regulations: Initiative 428 (2020) capped payday and similar short-term loans at 36% APR.
The general usury cap is 16% for most consumer loans.
Licensed delayed deposit services must comply with the 36% APR cap.
The Nebraska Consumer Protection Act prohibits deceptive lending practices.
NE.consumer_rights_summary: Nebraska voters approved a 36% APR cap on payday loans in 2020, dramatically reducing high-cost lending in the state. The general usury cap is 16% for consumer loans. The Department of Banking and Finance regulates consumer lenders, and complaints can be filed with the Department or the Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit services organizations must provide a written contract detailing all services, fees, and the consumer's right to cancel within 3 business days
Prohibition on charging fees before services are actually delivered to the consumer
Credit repair companies must disclose that consumers have the right to dispute credit reporting errors directly and for free
Prohibition on making any guarantees about credit repair outcomes or credit score improvement
Organizations must not misrepresent their services or affiliation with credit bureaus or government agencies
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Nebraska Department of Banking and Finance

personal_loans.rate_cap: 16% APR general usury cap
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans are regulated under Nebraska's general usury laws (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 45-101 to 45-104). Lenders must be licensed by the Department of Banking and Finance if they make consumer loans.

payday_loans.rate_cap: 36% APR cap (Initiative 428, 2020); includes all fees and charges
payday_loans.notes: Licensed delayed deposit services must comply with the 36% APR cap established by voter initiative in 2020. This is a dramatic reduction from the previous unregulated market. Nebraska maintains a statewide database to track outstanding payday loans and prevent loan stacking.

title_loans.notes: Title loans are not explicitly licensed or regulated in Nebraska and are generally not permitted under state law.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 16% APR general usury cap applies; however, licensed consumer finance lenders may charge higher rates under specific statutory provisions (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 45-101.02)
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are regulated under Nebraska's consumer finance laws. Licensed lenders can charge rates above 16% under the consumer finance lender exemption, but must comply with licensing and disclosure requirements.

mortgage.notes: Nebraska mortgage lending is regulated by the Department of Banking and Finance. Mortgages are primarily foreclosed through judicial process (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 80-101 to 80-2365). Lenders must comply with federal regulations including RESPA, TILA, and ECOA. Nebraska requires licensing for mortgage loan originators under the Nationwide Mortgage Licensing System (NMLS).

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The federal Military Lending Act (10 U.S.C. § 987) caps interest rates on covered borrower loans at 36% MAPR (Military Annual Percentage Rate), including all fees and charges. This applies to active duty service members and dependents. The 36% MAPR cap is stricter than Nebraska's general 16% cap for consumer loans, and Nebraska's 36% payday loan cap aligns with this federal floor for certain loans.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3701 et seq.) provides active duty service members with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, relief from default judgments, stay of foreclosure and eviction proceedings for up to 9 months, and the right to terminate residential leases with 30 days' notice. These protections apply in Nebraska and can be enforced in state and federal courts.
state_specific_protections: Nebraska recognizes military service credit for state employment and licensing purposes (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 84-901 et seq.)
Property tax exemptions available for disabled veterans (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 77-3503.01)
Veteran business loan and grant programs administered through the Nebraska Department of Economic Development
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: VA loans are available in Nebraska with no state-specific restrictions. Nebraska recognizes VA home loans as meeting mortgage underwriting standards. The state does not impose additional state mortgage licensing requirements beyond federal and NMLS standards for VA-backed loans.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Offutt Air Force Base (Bellevue, near Omaha) is the primary military installation in Nebraska and home to U.S. Strategic Command. Military Lending Act enforcement efforts focus on this area.

complaint_resources.name: Nebraska Department of Banking and Finance
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints regarding licensed lenders, payday loan providers, mortgage lenders, and credit repair organizations. Enforces state lending and usury laws.

complaint_resources.name: Nebraska Attorney General - Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Investigates complaints related to deceptive lending practices, credit repair fraud, predatory lending, and violations of the Nebraska Consumer Protection Act (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 87-301 et seq.).

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal consumer complaint portal for issues with mortgages, payday loans, personal loans, credit reporting, and other consumer financial products. CFPB has jurisdiction over federal lending laws.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints regarding credit repair fraud, identity theft, deceptive practices, and violations of the Credit Repair Organizations Act.

complaint_resources.name: Nebraska Better Business Bureau
complaint_resources.description: Provides consumer complaint resolution services and business accreditation information for lending and credit service providers.

statute_links.name: Nebraska Credit Services Organization Act (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 45-801 to 45-815)
statute_links.description: Establishes licensing, bonding, and conduct requirements for credit repair companies and credit services organizations in Nebraska.

statute_links.name: Initiative 428 (2020) - Payday Loan APR Cap (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 45-101.02)
statute_links.description: Capped payday and short-term loans at 36% APR, approved by Nebraska voters in 2020. Establishes maximum loan amount of $500 and requires database tracking.

statute_links.name: Nebraska Usury Laws (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 45-101 to 45-104)
statute_links.description: General usury cap of 16% APR for consumer loans, with certain exemptions for licensed lenders. Defines criminal penalties for usury violations.

statute_links.name: Nebraska Consumer Protection Act (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 87-301 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Prohibits deceptive, unfair, and unconscionable practices in consumer transactions, including lending. Provides enforcement mechanisms and consumer remedies.

statute_links.name: Nebraska Mortgage Lending and Servicer Standards (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 81-101 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Regulates mortgage loan originators, lenders, and servicers. Requires licensing and compliance with federal mortgage standards.

statute_links.name: Nebraska Foreclosure Laws (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 80-101 to 80-2365)
statute_links.description: Governs judicial foreclosure process for mortgages and other secured debts. Requires judicial process; non-judicial foreclosure is not permitted.

statute_links.name: Military Lending Act (10 U.S.C. § 987)
statute_links.description: Federal law capping interest rates on covered borrower loans at 36% MAPR. Applies nationwide to active duty service members and certain dependents.

statute_links.name: Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3701 et seq.)
statute_links.description: Federal law providing active duty service members with 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, foreclosure relief, and other protections.

NE.recent_changes: Initiative 428, approved by Nebraska voters in 2020, represents the most significant recent change, reducing the payday loan APR cap from unregulated to 36% APR, with a $500 maximum loan amount and mandatory database tracking. No major statutory changes to payday or personal lending regulations have occurred in 2024-2025.

NV.name: Nevada
NV.usury_cap: No general usury cap; payday loans legal with no rate cap (term and amount limits apply)
NV.consumer_protection_agency: Nevada Attorney General Bureau of Consumer Protection
key_regulations: Payday loans capped at 25% of borrower's expected gross monthly income.
No APR cap on payday loans; rates can exceed 600% APR.
Maximum loan term is 35 days.
Payday lenders must be licensed and are regulated under the Deferred Deposit Loan Law.
NV.consumer_rights_summary: Nevada allows payday lending with no interest rate cap, though loan amounts are limited to 25% of gross monthly income. The lack of rate caps means APRs can be extremely high. The Financial Institutions Division regulates consumer lenders, and consumers can file complaints with the Division or the Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit repair companies must provide written contract with clear terms, cost, timeline, and explicit statement that consumer has right to dispute debts independently at no cost
Companies cannot charge fees until services are actually performed; upfront fees are prohibited
Must provide consumer with copy of their credit report at no charge and inform them of right to obtain free report directly from bureaus
Cannot make false or misleading claims about ability to remove accurate negative information from credit reports
Must disclose the consumer's right to cancel the contract within 5 business days without penalty or obligation
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Nevada Division of Mortgage Lending

personal_loans.rate_cap: No general usury cap on personal loans under Nevada law; rates determined by contract
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans are largely unregulated in Nevada absent specific licensing requirements; lenders and borrowers may negotiate rates freely

payday_loans.rate_cap: No APR cap; rates can exceed 600% APR
payday_loans.max_amount: 25% of borrower's expected gross monthly income
payday_loans.notes: Regulated under Nev. Rev. Stat. § 604A.200-604A.215 (Deferred Deposit Loan Law); lenders must be licensed; loans capped at 25% of gross monthly income with maximum 35-day term; rollovers and extensions allowed but subject to income limits

title_loans.rate_cap: No rate cap; governed by negotiated contract terms
title_loans.notes: Title loans are legal in Nevada but minimally regulated; lenders should comply with general consumer lending disclosures under Nevada law

installment_loans.rate_cap: No general usury cap; rates negotiated between lender and borrower
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are permitted; lenders making installment loans of $2,500 or more must be licensed under Nev. Rev. Stat. § 604A; Truth in Lending Act (TILA) and Regulation Z disclosures apply to federally-regulated lenders

mortgage.notes: Nevada is a non-judicial foreclosure state; foreclosure by sale is permitted without court involvement if deed of trust contains power of sale clause. Residential mortgage lenders must be licensed by Nevada Financial Institutions Division. Nevada Revised Statutes § 107.010-107.480 govern mortgages and deeds of trust. Borrowers have right to cure default before foreclosure sale; homestead exemption available for primary residence (limited). Reverse mortgages regulated under federal HOEPA standards.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) caps the annual percentage rate on consumer credit extended to covered borrowers at 36% MAPR (Military Annual Percentage Rate), including all fees and interest. Covered borrowers are active-duty service members and their dependents.
