Title lending is also restricted under state law.
PA.consumer_rights_summary: Pennsylvania effectively bans payday lending through its strict usury laws. Licensed consumer discount companies can charge higher rates but remain well below payday loan levels. Consumers can file complaints with the Department of Banking and Securities or the Attorney General's Bureau of Consumer Protection.

key_provisions: Credit services organizations must provide written contracts in plain language disclosing all material terms and conditions
Prohibition on charging fees before performing promised services; all fees must be fully earned
Cooling-off period of 5 days from contract date to cancel without penalty or obligation
Prohibition on making false or misleading claims about ability to improve credit standing or remove accurate negative information
Required to provide copies of credit reports and explain consumer rights under federal law
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Pennsylvania Department of Banking and Securities

personal_loans.rate_cap: 6% APR for non-licensed lenders; 24% APR for licensed consumer discount companies
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans are regulated under Pennsylvania usury laws. Licensed consumer discount companies must comply with the Consumer Discount Company Act and are subject to licensing by the Department of Banking and Securities.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Effectively banned through usury cap of 6% for non-licensed lenders
payday_loans.notes: Pennsylvania bans payday lending. The state's strict usury laws (6% cap for non-licensed lenders) make payday lending economically unfeasible. No licensing exists for payday lenders in Pennsylvania.

title_loans.notes: Title lending is restricted under Pennsylvania state law. The usury caps and consumer protection laws effectively prohibit traditional title lending operations.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 6% APR for non-licensed lenders; 24% APR for licensed small loan/consumer discount companies
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are permitted and regulated under the Consumer Discount Company Act. Licensed lenders can charge up to 24% APR and must be regulated by the Pennsylvania Department of Banking and Securities.

mortgage.notes: Pennsylvania mortgages are subject to state and federal regulations. Foreclosure in Pennsylvania is judicial, requiring court proceedings. Lenders must comply with the Mortgage Forgiveness Debt Relief Act and federal TILA/RESPA requirements. Licensed mortgage lenders are regulated by the Department of Banking and Securities. Right-to-cure periods and anti-deficiency protections exist under state law.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The federal Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) applies to all service members and their dependents in Pennsylvania. It establishes a 36% Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) cap, prohibits mandatory arbitration and class action waivers in certain military lending products, restricts certain debt collection practices, and requires clear disclosure of all loan terms.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) provides service members with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, protections against foreclosure and eviction without court order during active duty, extended lease termination rights, and protection from default judgments without proper notice. These protections apply to active duty service members in Pennsylvania.
state_specific_protections: Pennsylvania Property Tax/Rent Rebate Program available to veterans and surviving spouses meeting income and property ownership requirements
Veterans are exempt from Pennsylvania property taxes on their primary residence if they have service-connected disabilities rated at 100% by the VA
Pennsylvania Military Family Relief Commission provides emergency financial assistance to Pennsylvania National Guard members and their families during state active duty
State bonus programs for veterans and specific educational benefits administered through the Pennsylvania Higher Education Assistance Agency (PHEAA)
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Pennsylvania has a robust VA home loan program through the Pennsylvania Housing Finance Agency. Veterans may qualify for VA loans with no down payment. The state also offers property tax exemptions for disabled veterans (100% service-connected disability rating) on their primary residence. PA Housing Finance Agency administers programs supporting veteran homeownership and provides resources for VA loan information.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort Indiantown Gap (Lebanon County) - Pennsylvania Army National Guard installation; Naval Station Philadelphia (inactive but historically significant); multiple recruiting stations and reserve component facilities throughout the state

complaint_resources.name: Pennsylvania Department of Banking and Securities
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints regarding licensed lenders, credit services organizations, and consumer finance companies. Manages licensing and enforcement of lending and credit repair laws.

complaint_resources.name: Pennsylvania Attorney General - Bureau of Consumer Protection
complaint_resources.description: Investigates consumer complaints involving deceptive lending practices, unfair trade practices, and violations of the Pennsylvania Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Law.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints regarding credit repair scams, deceptive lending practices, and identity theft. Shares information with state attorneys general.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about consumer finance products and services, including lending and credit services. Has enforcement authority over certain lenders.

statute_links.name: Pennsylvania Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Comprehensive law regulating credit repair companies, including bonding requirements, contract terms, fee restrictions, and cancellation rights.

statute_links.name: Pennsylvania Consumer Discount Company Act
statute_links.description: Governs licensed consumer discount and small loan companies, establishing rate caps, licensing requirements, and consumer protections.

statute_links.name: Pennsylvania Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Law
statute_links.description: Prohibits deceptive, unfair, and fraudulent lending practices; provides remedies for consumers harmed by violations.

statute_links.name: Pennsylvania Usury Laws (Title 41)
statute_links.description: Establishes the 6% usury cap for non-licensed lenders and governs interest rate limitations throughout Pennsylvania.

statute_links.name: Pennsylvania Mortgage Licensing Law
statute_links.description: Regulates mortgage lenders and brokers, requiring licensing and compliance with state and federal mortgage regulations.

PA.recent_changes: No significant changes to Pennsylvania lending or credit repair laws in 2024-2025. The state continues to maintain its strict usury caps and effective payday lending ban through existing statutory framework. Regulatory agencies continue enforcement of existing consumer protections.

RI.name: Rhode Island
RI.usury_cap: 21% (36% for licensed small loan lenders); payday loans legal at 10% fee per $100
RI.consumer_protection_agency: Rhode Island Attorney General Consumer Protection Unit
key_regulations: Payday loans capped at $500 with maximum fee of 10% of the loan amount.
Maximum loan term is 13 days.
Borrowers may have up to three outstanding loans at a time.
Licensed lenders regulated under the Rhode Island Small Loan Act.
RI.consumer_rights_summary: Rhode Island allows payday lending with a $500 cap and 10% fee limit. Borrowers can have up to three outstanding loans simultaneously, which increases debt risk. The Division of Banking regulates consumer lenders, and complaints can be filed with the Division or the Attorney General.

credit_repair_laws.state_statute: Rhode Island Credit Services Organization Act
key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide a written contract before charging any fees or requesting payment
Contracts must include itemized description of services, total cost, payment terms, and cancellation rights
Credit repair companies cannot guarantee removal of accurate negative information from credit reports
Companies must disclose consumer rights under the Fair Credit Reporting Act and right to dispute directly with credit bureaus at no cost
Prohibition on collecting fees before services are actually performed
Right to cancel contract within 5 business days without penalty
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Rhode Island Division of Banking

personal_loans.rate_cap: 21% APR maximum for unlicensed lenders; 36% APR for licensed small loan lenders
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans regulated under Rhode Island usury laws and the Small Loan Act. Licensed lenders can charge up to 36% APR. Regulated by the Division of Banking.

payday_loans.rate_cap: 10% fee per $100 borrowed (equivalent to approximately 260% APR on a 14-day loan)
payday_loans.notes: Payday loans capped at $500 with maximum fee of 10% of loan amount. Maximum loan term is 13 days. Borrowers may have up to three outstanding loans at a time. Licensed lenders regulated under the Rhode Island Small Loan Act. No mandatory statewide database tracking system in place.

title_loans.notes: Title loans are not explicitly legalized in Rhode Island. The state does not have a statutory framework authorizing vehicle title lending.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 21% APR maximum for unlicensed lenders; 36% APR for licensed small loan lenders under the Small Loan Act
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are governed by the Rhode Island Small Loan Act (R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-24-1 et seq.) and general usury laws. Licensed lenders must comply with consumer protection requirements and licensing under the Division of Banking.

mortgage.notes: Mortgages regulated under R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-27 (Residential Mortgage Lending Act). Foreclosures are judicial in Rhode Island, requiring court proceedings. Mortgage lenders and servicers must be licensed by the Division of Banking. State requires residential mortgage licensees to maintain net worth requirements and comply with fair lending laws. Borrowers have right to cure before foreclosure judgment.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) applies to all service members and their dependents in Rhode Island, capping the Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) at 36% on covered consumer credit (payday loans, title loans, installment loans, and overdraft services). Covered borrowers must receive mandatory disclosures. Military lenders must be registered with the Department of Defense.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) provides active duty service members in Rhode Island with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, protection from foreclosure and eviction during active duty, right to terminate certain contracts and leases, and extension of deadlines for legal proceedings.
state_specific_protections: Rhode Island recognizes and enforces federal Military Lending Act protections for all covered borrowers
State usury caps (21% for general lending, 36% for licensed small loan lenders) do not apply to service members, who receive the more favorable 36% MAPR cap under the MLA
Rhode Island Division of Banking investigates complaints from service members regarding lending violations
State requires lenders to verify borrower military status and comply with MLA disclosure requirements
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Rhode Island Housing & Mortgage Finance Corporation administers VA loan programs. State offers property tax exemptions for veterans with service-connected disabilities (R.I. Gen. Laws § 44-3-1 et seq.). No statewide VA loan-specific interest rate caps beyond federal requirements, but VA loans benefit from federal guaranty and favorable terms through approved VA lenders.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Naval Station Newport (Newport Naval Base), Naval Undersea Warfare Center Division Newport, and Fort Adams State Park area military facility.

complaint_resources.name: Rhode Island Division of Banking
complaint_resources.description: Regulates licensed lenders, credit repair organizations, mortgage lenders, and consumer finance companies. Handles complaints regarding predatory lending, licensing violations, and consumer protection act violations.

complaint_resources.name: Rhode Island Attorney General Consumer Protection Unit
complaint_resources.description: Investigates consumer complaints, enforces consumer protection laws including credit services organization violations, deceptive trade practices, and lending law violations. Maintains public complaint database.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about payday loans, personal loans, credit reporting, credit repair services, mortgages, and other consumer financial products. Investigates violations of federal lending laws.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Enforces federal credit services organization law and credit repair organizations act. Accepts complaints about credit repair scams and predatory lending practices.

complaint_resources.name: National Credit Union Administration (NCUA)
complaint_resources.description: Regulates federal credit unions operating in Rhode Island and accepts complaints regarding credit union lending practices.

statute_links.name: Rhode Island Small Loan Act
statute_links.description: R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-24-1 et seq. Governs licensed small loan lenders, sets 36% APR cap for licensed lenders, establishes licensing and consumer protection requirements.

statute_links.name: Rhode Island Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-25-1 et seq. Regulates credit repair organizations, requires bonding, registration, written contracts, prohibits upfront fees, and provides cancellation rights.

statute_links.name: Rhode Island Usury Laws
statute_links.description: R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-11-1 et seq. Sets 21% APR maximum interest rate cap for unlicensed lenders and general consumer credit.

statute_links.name: Rhode Island Residential Mortgage Lending Act
statute_links.description: R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-27-1 et seq. Regulates residential mortgage lenders, loan originators, and servicers operating in Rhode Island. Includes licensing, net worth, and consumer protection requirements.

statute_links.name: Rhode Island Payday Loan Regulations
statute_links.description: Regulatory guidance from the Division of Banking on payday loan licensing, $500 maximum amount, 10% fee cap, 13-day maximum term, and three-loan outstanding limit.

statute_links.name: Rhode Island Foreclosure Law
statute_links.description: R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-27-4.1 et seq. Establishes judicial foreclosure requirements, borrower notification requirements, and right to cure before judgment.

statute_links.name: Rhode Island Deceptive Trade Practices Act
statute_links.description: R.I. Gen. Laws § 6-13.1-1 et seq. Prohibits unfair and deceptive trade practices in consumer transactions, enforced by the Attorney General.

RI.recent_changes: Rhode Island enacted updates to its Small Loan Act regulations in recent years to modernize licensing requirements for online lenders and strengthen underwriting standards. The Division of Banking has increased enforcement activity on payday lending compliance, particularly regarding the three-loan maximum outstanding requirement and fee cap violations. No major legislative changes to the usury cap or payday loan framework occurred in 2024-2025.

SC.name: South Carolina
SC.usury_cap: 8.75% default rate; payday loans capped at $550 with 15% fee
SC.consumer_protection_agency: South Carolina Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
SC.licensing_board: South Carolina Board of Financial Institutions
key_regulations: Payday loans (deferred presentment) capped at $550 with maximum fee of 15% of the advance.
Borrowers limited to one outstanding loan at a time tracked via statewide database.
A one-day cooling-off period required between loans.
Lenders must offer an extended payment plan at no additional cost after the fourth consecutive loan.
SC.consumer_rights_summary: South Carolina allows payday lending with a $550 cap, 15% fee limit, and a statewide database to prevent multiple simultaneous loans. After four consecutive loans, borrowers must be offered an extended payment plan at no extra cost. Complaints can be filed with the Board of Financial Institutions or the Attorney General.

credit_repair_laws.state_statute: South Carolina Credit Services Organization Act
key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide written contract with detailed explanation of services, fees, and timeline before charging consumer
Prohibition on making false or misleading claims about credit repair company's ability to improve credit or remove negative information
Requirement to disclose consumer's right to dispute items with credit reporting agencies directly at no cost
Credit repair services cannot commence until after a 5-business-day waiting period following contract execution

personal_loans.rate_cap: 8.75% APR (default usury cap); rates may vary based on loan type and creditor classification
personal_loans.notes: South Carolina enforces an 8.75% default usury cap for consumer loans, though certain exemptions may apply to licensed lenders and specific loan types. Rates must be clearly disclosed.

payday_loans.rate_cap: 15% maximum fee (not APR) on advance amount
payday_loans.notes: Payday loans (deferred presentment) capped at $550 with 15% maximum fee. Borrowers limited to one outstanding loan at a time via statewide database. One-day cooling-off period required between loans. After four consecutive loans, lenders must offer extended payment plan at no additional cost.

title_loans.notes: South Carolina does not permit vehicle title loans as a regulated lending product. Such transactions may violate usury laws or other consumer protection statutes.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 8.75% usury cap applies unless lender is exempt; rates must be disclosed in accordance with Truth in Lending Act (Regulation Z)
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are permitted and governed by South Carolina's general usury laws and federal consumer protection statutes. Interest rates and terms must be disclosed clearly to borrowers.

mortgage.notes: Mortgages in South Carolina are regulated under state and federal law. Foreclosures are judicial proceedings (require court action). South Carolina has licensing requirements for mortgage lenders and brokers under the Mortgage Lending Act (S.C. Code Ann. § 40-58-10 et seq.). The state is a 'title theory' state where the lender holds title until the debt is satisfied. Homeowners have statutory redemption rights post-foreclosure in limited circumstances.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) applies to all service members and their dependents nationwide. It imposes a 36% MAPR (Military Annual Percentage Rate) cap on covered borrowing and prohibits certain terms and practices (mandatory arbitration, wage assignment, etc.). South Carolina lenders must comply with MLA requirements.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953 et seq.) protects active-duty service members by capping interest on pre-service debts at 6%, preventing foreclosure/eviction without court order, and allowing lease termination for military-related relocation. South Carolina courts must recognize and enforce SCRA protections.
state_specific_protections: South Carolina does not impose additional rate caps beyond federal MLA requirements for military borrowers
State licensing and enforcement by South Carolina Board of Financial Institutions includes oversight of compliance with MLA provisions by payday and other regulated lenders
Veterans may access South Carolina Veterans' Trust Fund for financial assistance, administered by the Department of Veterans' Affairs
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: South Carolina participates in VA loan programs. The state does not impose a property tax on active-duty military personnel's homestead property. Veterans may qualify for property tax exemptions on their primary residence if they meet disability or service-connected criteria. South Carolina Housing Finance and Development Authority (SCHOUSING) offers programs to support veteran homeownership.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort Jackson (Columbia); Naval Station Charleston; Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island (Beaufort); Fort Liberty nearby in North Carolina (regional presence)

complaint_resources.name: South Carolina Board of Financial Institutions
complaint_resources.description: Licenses and regulates payday lenders, mortgage lenders, and other financial service providers. Handles complaints related to regulated lender conduct and licensing violations.

complaint_resources.name: South Carolina Attorney General — Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Investigates consumer complaints related to unfair/deceptive lending practices, credit repair fraud, and violations of consumer protection statutes. Enforces state consumer laws.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about payday loans, mortgages, personal loans, and other consumer financial products. Investigates violations of federal consumer protection laws (TILA, FCRA, etc.).

complaint_resources.name: South Carolina Department of Consumer Affairs
complaint_resources.description: Provides consumer information and assistance; receives complaints about consumer scams and unfair trade practices.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about fraud, scams, identity theft, and deceptive practices related to credit repair and lending.

statute_links.name: South Carolina Code of Laws, Title 37 (Consumer Protection)
statute_links.description: Comprehensive state consumer protection laws including usury limits, payday lending regulations, credit services organizations, and debt collection practices.

statute_links.name: S.C. Code Ann. § 37-3-501 et seq. (Payday Lending/Deferred Presentment)
statute_links.description: Primary statute governing payday loans, including $550 cap, 15% fee limit, statewide database, cooling-off period, and extended payment plan requirements.

statute_links.name: S.C. Code Ann. § 37-14-10 et seq. (Credit Services Organization Act)
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair organizations, prohibits upfront fees, requires written contracts, and mandates 5-business-day cancellation period.

statute_links.name: S.C. Code Ann. § 40-58-10 et seq. (Mortgage Lending Act)
statute_links.description: Licensing and regulation of mortgage lenders and brokers in South Carolina.

statute_links.name: S.C. Code Ann. § 34-36-100 et seq. (Debt Collection)
statute_links.description: State-level debt collection protections; complements federal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA).

SC.recent_changes: No significant changes to South Carolina payday lending or credit repair laws reported in 2024-2025. The regulatory framework established in prior years remains in effect, with enforcement by the Board of Financial Institutions and Attorney General's office continuing.

SD.name: South Dakota
SD.usury_cap: 36% APR cap on all consumer loans (Initiated Measure 21, 2016)
SD.consumer_protection_agency: South Dakota Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
key_regulations: Initiated Measure 21 (2016) capped all consumer loans at 36% APR, effectively banning payday lending.
Prior to 2016, South Dakota had no usury cap and was a hub for payday lenders.
The Division of Banking enforces the rate cap on all licensed lenders.
Violations are subject to criminal penalties including Class 1 misdemeanor charges.
SD.consumer_rights_summary: South Dakota voters approved a 36% APR cap on all consumer loans in 2016, reversing the state's previous reputation as having no usury limit. This effectively banned payday lending. Consumers can file complaints with the Division of Banking or the Attorney General's Consumer Protection Division.

credit_repair_laws.state_statute: South Dakota Codified Laws Chapter 37-14 (Credit Services Organization Act)
key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide written contract with clear itemization of services and costs before charging fees
Prohibition on making false or misleading claims about ability to repair credit or remove accurate negative information
Requirement to disclose consumer rights under federal Fair Credit Reporting Act and state law
Written notice of cancellation rights must be provided; consumer may cancel within 3 business days without penalty
Prohibition on collection of fees before services are substantially performed
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: South Dakota Division of Banking (oversight/enforcement)

personal_loans.rate_cap: 36% APR maximum (Initiated Measure 21, 2016)
personal_loans.notes: All consumer loans, including personal loans, are subject to the 36% APR usury cap. Loans must comply with Division of Banking licensing and reporting requirements. The cap applies to all lenders including banks, credit unions, and online lenders operating in South Dakota.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Effectively banned by 36% APR usury cap
payday_loans.notes: Payday loans are prohibited in South Dakota. Initiated Measure 21 (2016) established a 36% APR cap on all consumer loans, making traditional payday lending economically unfeasible. Prior to 2016, South Dakota had no usury limit and was a major hub for payday lending operations. Any lender charging rates exceeding 36% APR is subject to criminal penalties.

title_loans.notes: While title loans are not explicitly prohibited by statute, they are effectively regulated under the same 36% APR usury cap as all consumer loans. The cap restricts traditional title lending models that typically charge rates far exceeding 36% APR. Any title loan offered in South Dakota must comply with the rate cap.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 36% APR maximum (Initiated Measure 21, 2016)
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are legal and commonly offered by licensed lenders. All installment loans must comply with the 36% APR usury cap regardless of loan amount or term. Lenders must be licensed by the Division of Banking and comply with all applicable disclosure and reporting requirements.

mortgage.notes: South Dakota mortgages are regulated under common law and state statutes (SDCL Title 32). South Dakota permits non-judicial foreclosure through power-of-sale clauses in mortgages; judicial foreclosure is also available. Lenders must be licensed mortgage lenders under SDCL § 32-4A. The 36% APR usury cap does not apply to mortgages secured by real property. South Dakota has streamlined foreclosure procedures but requires notice to borrower and compliance with federal servicing standards.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The federal Military Lending Act (MLA) establishes a 36% Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) cap on covered borrower loans. This applies to all active duty service members and their dependents in South Dakota. The MLA prohibits mandatory arbitration, limits fees, requires specific disclosures, and restricts certain loan terms. South Dakota's 36% APR usury cap aligns with and complements MLA protections.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA) provides protected service members with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, foreclosure and eviction protections, lease termination rights, and protection from default judgments without court appearance. Service members can request SCRA protections upon providing military status documentation. South Dakota courts are required to honor SCRA protections.
state_specific_protections: South Dakota's 36% APR usury cap provides baseline protection that exceeds MLA requirements, protecting all consumers including active duty and reserve military members from predatory lending
South Dakota does not prohibit payday lending specifically for military members (as some states do) because payday lending is banned for all consumers under Initiated Measure 21
Military members may file complaints with the South Dakota Division of Banking or Attorney General Consumer Protection Division regarding lending violations
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: South Dakota recognizes VA loans for home purchase. The state does not impose additional restrictions on VA loans beyond federal VA requirements. South Dakota offers property tax exemptions for eligible veterans (SDCL § 10-4-12 and § 10-4-13), including disabled veterans. The South Dakota Veterans Benefits Office (part of Department of Military and Veteran Affairs) provides information on state veteran housing benefits and programs.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Ellsworth Space Force Base (near Rapid City) is the primary military installation in South Dakota. This base is a focus area for Military Lending Act enforcement by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau.

complaint_resources.name: South Dakota Division of Banking
complaint_resources.description: Licenses and regulates consumer lenders, mortgage lenders, and credit services organizations. Enforces the 36% APR usury cap and investigates lending violations and unlicensed lending activities.

complaint_resources.name: South Dakota Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Investigates consumer complaints regarding unfair or deceptive lending practices, payday lending violations, credit repair scams, and other consumer finance violations. Accepts complaints online and by phone.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal agency accepting complaints about credit products, loans, and financial services. Investigates violations of federal lending laws including the Military Lending Act and Truth in Lending Act.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about credit repair scams, identity theft, predatory lending, and other consumer finance fraud.

statute_links.name: South Dakota Initiated Measure 21 (2016) - 36% APR Usury Cap
statute_links.description: The foundational law establishing a 36% APR maximum rate on all consumer loans in South Dakota, effectively banning payday lending and capping rates on personal, installment, and title loans.

statute_links.name: South Dakota Codified Laws § 37-14 (Credit Services Organization Act)
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair organizations, requiring written contracts, prohibition of upfront fees, and 3-day cancellation rights for consumers.

statute_links.name: South Dakota Codified Laws § 32-4A (Mortgage Lender Licensing)
statute_links.description: Establishes licensing requirements and regulatory framework for mortgage lenders in South Dakota.

statute_links.name: South Dakota Codified Laws Title 45 (South Dakota Uniform Commercial Code - Articles 3, 9)
statute_links.description: Governs promissory notes, personal property security interests, and installment loan documentation.

statute_links.name: South Dakota Codified Laws § 10-4-12 and § 10-4-13 (Veteran Property Tax Exemptions)
statute_links.description: Provides property tax exemption information for eligible veterans and disabled veterans in South Dakota.

SD.recent_changes: No significant changes to South Dakota consumer lending or credit repair legislation in 2024-2025. The 36% APR usury cap remains the dominant regulatory framework established by Initiated Measure 21 (2016). Ongoing enforcement by the Division of Banking and Attorney General continues to address payday lending and predatory lending violations.

TN.name: Tennessee
TN.usury_cap: 24% for consumer finance loans; payday loans regulated under Deferred Presentment Act
TN.consumer_protection_agency: Tennessee Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
key_regulations: Payday loans (deferred presentment) capped at $500 with maximum fee of 15% of the advance.
Maximum loan term is 31 days.
Borrowers limited to two outstanding payday loans at a time.
The Tennessee Consumer Protection Act provides additional safeguards against deceptive lending.
TN.consumer_rights_summary: Tennessee allows payday lending with a $500 cap and 15% fee limit. Borrowers are limited to two simultaneous loans. The Department of Financial Institutions regulates all consumer lenders, and complaints can be filed with the Department or the Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit repair companies must provide written contract clearly stating all services, costs, and cancellation terms before charging any fees
Prohibited from making false or misleading claims about ability to improve credit reports or remove accurate negative information
Required to disclose that consumers have right to dispute credit report items directly with credit bureaus at no cost
Must inform clients that credit repair outcomes cannot be guaranteed and improvement takes time
Prohibited from advising clients to dispute accurate items or engage in deceptive practices
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Tennessee Department of Financial Institutions

personal_loans.rate_cap: 24% APR for consumer finance loans; rates negotiable for other personal loans
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans regulated under Tennessee Consumer Finance Act (Tenn. Code Ann. § 45-1-201 et seq.). Lenders must be licensed by Tennessee Department of Financial Institutions unless exempt.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Maximum fee of 15% of the advance amount
payday_loans.notes: Regulated under Tennessee Deferred Presentment Act (Tenn. Code Ann. § 45-17-101 et seq.). Borrowers limited to two outstanding payday loans at any time. Lenders must be licensed with Tennessee Department of Financial Institutions. No statewide database system currently in place.

title_loans.notes: Title loans are not explicitly permitted under Tennessee law. Any title-based lending arrangement must comply with general usury and consumer lending regulations.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 24% APR for consumer finance installment loans; rates may vary for other installment loans
installment_loans.notes: Governed by Tennessee Consumer Finance Act (Tenn. Code Ann. § 45-1-201 et seq.). Lenders must disclose all terms including finance charges, payment schedule, and total amount financed.

mortgage.notes: Tennessee mortgages are governed by Tenn. Code Ann. § 35-5-101 et seq. Foreclosures are non-judicial (power of sale) when property contains a power of sale clause in the deed of trust; judicial foreclosure available as alternative. Lenders must comply with federal Truth in Lending Act (TILA), Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA), and Fair Lending laws. Mortgage lenders and loan servicers must be licensed by Tennessee Department of Financial Institutions.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) applies in Tennessee and caps the Annual Percentage Rate (APR) at 36% for covered borrowers on covered loans, including payday loans, vehicle title loans, and tax refund loans. Covered borrowers include active duty service members and their dependents. Tennessee payday loans at 15% fee are well below the 36% MAPR cap.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953 et seq.) provides active duty service members with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, protection from foreclosure and eviction during military service, and lease termination rights without penalty. These protections apply in Tennessee courts regardless of state law.
state_specific_protections: Tennessee does not impose additional rate caps or lending restrictions beyond federal protections for military members, as the 24% consumer finance cap already exceeds the federal 36% MLA threshold
Service members can file complaints with Tennessee Department of Financial Institutions if lenders fail to comply with Military Lending Act requirements
Tennessee recognizes SCRA protections in state courts and civil proceedings
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Tennessee participates in VA home loan programs. Property Tax Assessment Limitation for Disabled Veterans: Tennessee provides a property tax exemption on up to $175,000 of assessed value for 100% disabled veterans (Tenn. Code Ann. § 26-2-301). Tennessee Housing Development Agency (THDA) offers down payment and closing cost assistance programs for eligible borrowers including veterans.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Major military installations in Tennessee include: Naval Support Activity Mid-South (Millington), Fort Campbell (partial; borders Kentucky), Arnold Engineering Development Complex (AEDC, Tullahoma), Fort Rucker (Enterprise, Alabama but Tennessee proximity), and Volunteer Training Site (minor installations). These areas warrant focused Military Lending Act enforcement attention.

complaint_resources.name: Tennessee Department of Financial Institutions
complaint_resources.description: Primary regulator for payday lenders, consumer finance companies, credit services organizations, and mortgage lenders. Accepts complaints regarding licensing violations, deceptive practices, and regulatory non-compliance.

complaint_resources.name: Tennessee Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Enforces Tennessee Consumer Protection Act (Tenn. Code Ann. § 47-18-101 et seq.). Investigates complaints of deceptive lending practices, credit repair fraud, payday loan violations, and unlicensed lending.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about payday loans, consumer finance companies, mortgage lenders, credit reporting agencies, and other financial products. Investigates violations of federal lending laws.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Investigates credit repair scams, deceptive lending practices, identity theft, and other consumer fraud affecting Tennessee residents.

statute_links.name: Tennessee Deferred Presentment Act
statute_links.description: Governs payday loan regulation including $500 cap, 15% fee limit, 31-day maximum term, and two-loan limit.

statute_links.name: Tennessee Consumer Finance Act
statute_links.description: Establishes 24% APR cap for consumer finance loans and licensing requirements for consumer lenders.

statute_links.name: Tennessee Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair companies including bonding, registration, upfront fee prohibitions, and cancellation rights.

statute_links.name: Tennessee Consumer Protection Act
statute_links.description: Provides general consumer protections against deceptive lending practices and unfair trade practices.

statute_links.name: Tennessee Mortgage Foreclosure Law
statute_links.description: Governs non-judicial foreclosure procedures, power of sale, notice requirements, and redemption rights.

statute_links.name: Tennessee Property Tax Exemption for Disabled Veterans
statute_links.description: Provides property tax assessment exemption up to $175,000 for 100% service-connected disabled veterans.

TN.recent_changes: No significant changes to Tennessee consumer lending or credit repair legislation in 2024-2025. The regulatory framework remains stable under the existing Deferred Presentment Act, Consumer Finance Act, and Credit Services Organization Act, with ongoing enforcement by the Tennessee Department of Financial Institutions and Attorney General.

TX.name: Texas
TX.usury_cap: 10% for written contracts (18% default); payday/auto title loans regulated as credit access businesses
TX.consumer_protection_agency: Texas Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
TX.licensing_board: Texas Office of Consumer Credit Commissioner
key_regulations: Payday and auto title lenders operate as Credit Access Businesses (CABs) arranging loans through third-party lenders.
No state cap on CAB fees; effective APRs frequently exceed 500%.
Several cities (Austin, Dallas, San Antonio, Houston) have enacted local payday lending ordinances.
The Texas Finance Code regulates consumer lending but exempts CABs from rate caps.
TX.consumer_rights_summary: Texas allows payday and auto title lending through the Credit Access Business model, which lacks state-level fee caps. Several cities have enacted local ordinances to limit loan amounts and rollovers. Consumers can file complaints with the Office of Consumer Credit Commissioner or the Attorney General.

credit_repair_laws.statute_code: Tex. Fin. Code Ch. 393 (§ 393.001 et seq.)
key_provisions: Credit services organizations must provide consumers with a written contract before performing any services, detailing all terms and conditions
Prohibited from charging or collecting any fee or other consideration until the promised services have been fully performed
Must disclose all material terms in writing, including total cost, payment schedule, and estimated time to completion of services
Cannot make any claims or representations about improving a consumer's credit record that are not fully substantiated by reliable evidence
Must maintain a written compliance program and provide consumers with detailed account performance reporting
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Texas Secretary of State

personal_loans.rate_cap: 10% APR for written contracts; 18% APR default rate for oral or implied contracts (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 307.003)
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans are regulated under Texas Finance Code; rate caps apply to consumer loans not otherwise exempted

payday_loans.rate_cap: No state fee cap; structured through Credit Access Business (CAB) model with effective APRs frequently exceeding 500%
payday_loans.notes: Payday loans are legal in Texas but operated as Credit Access Businesses (CABs) that arrange loans through third-party lenders, exempting them from state usury rate caps. Several cities (Austin, Dallas, San Antonio, Houston) have enacted local ordinances imposing loan amount limits and rollover restrictions. Austin limits CAB loans to $1,500 and restricts rollovers; Dallas limits loans to $500 with 90-day mandatory waiting period between loans. The Texas Finance Code (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 59.001-59.060) regulates CABs but does not establish fee caps.

title_loans.rate_cap: No state cap; structured through CAB model with fees unregulated at state level
title_loans.notes: Auto title loans are legal but operated as Credit Access Businesses, exempt from state usury rate caps. Local ordinances in major cities may impose restrictions on loan amounts and terms.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 10% APR for written contracts; 18% APR default rate (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 307.003)
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are regulated under Texas Finance Code; same rate caps apply as personal loans for consumer lending transactions

mortgage.notes: Texas mortgages are primarily non-judicial foreclosure jurisdictions (power of sale); lenders must provide pre-foreclosure notice and right to cure. Texas Property Code § 51.001-51.0071 governs foreclosure procedures. Mortgage lending is regulated by the Texas Finance Commission and federal agencies (CFPB, OCC). Home equity lending is restricted to 80% LTV without mandatory arbitration provisions (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 50.001-50.0061). Texas allows one-to-four family residential mortgages; homestead exemptions available for primary residences.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The federal Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) caps military annual percentage rates (MAPRs) at 36% for covered borrowers (active duty service members and their dependents). This applies nationwide in Texas; lenders must verify military status and comply with disclosure requirements. The 36% cap covers interest, fees, and credit insurance.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3951 et seq.) provides active duty service members with protections including: a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, stay of legal proceedings during active duty, protection against default judgments, and foreclosure/eviction protections with mandatory notice requirements. Applies in Texas to all active duty service members.
state_specific_protections: Texas Property Code § 41.001 provides homestead exemptions for service members' primary residences, offering property tax relief and foreclosure protections
Texas Veterans Land Board administers low-interest land and home purchase loans for eligible veterans with competitive rates and flexible terms
Texas Service Member Family Protection Act (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 59.002) extends certain credit services protections to service members and their families
Texas Attorney General's office prioritizes enforcement of MLA violations and SCRA protections for military personnel stationed at state installations
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Texas has no state-specific VA loan restrictions. VA loans are federally guaranteed and available throughout Texas without additional state licensing requirements. The Texas Veterans Land Board is a state agency that provides supplemental veteran financing options with favorable terms for eligible Texas veterans.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort Bliss (El Paso, partially in New Mexico), Fort Hood (Killeen), Lackland Air Force Base (San Antonio), Naval Air Station Corpus Christi, Sheppard Air Force Base (Wichita Falls), Goodfellow Air Force Base (San Angelo), Ellington Field (Houston, Air Force Reserve)

complaint_resources.name: Texas Office of Consumer Credit Commissioner
complaint_resources.description: Primary regulator for payday lenders, credit access businesses, credit repair services, and consumer credit transactions in Texas; handles licensing, supervision, and consumer complaints

complaint_resources.name: Texas Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Enforces consumer protection laws, including deceptive lending practices, credit repair fraud, and unfair credit practices; accepts complaints and pursues enforcement actions

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal regulator accepting complaints about consumer finance products including payday loans, title loans, credit repair services, and other lending practices

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about credit repair fraud, predatory lending, and deceptive consumer finance practices

complaint_resources.name: Better Business Bureau (Texas)
complaint_resources.description: Consumer complaint database and dispute resolution for payday lenders, credit services, and other financial service providers

statute_links.name: Texas Finance Code Chapter 393 (Credit Services Organizations)
statute_links.description: Governs credit services organizations, requiring registration with the Secretary of State, bonding, written contracts, and prohibiting upfront fees.

statute_links.name: Texas Business & Commerce Code § 307 - Interest Rates
statute_links.description: Establishes usury rates (10% APR written contracts, 18% default) and exemptions for consumer credit transactions

statute_links.name: Texas Property Code § 51 - Residential Mortgage Foreclosure
statute_links.description: Governs non-judicial foreclosure procedures, notice requirements, and homeowner protections in mortgage default

statute_links.name: Texas Business & Commerce Code § 50 - Home Equity Lending
statute_links.description: Restricts home equity lending practices, prohibits mandatory arbitration, establishes 80% LTV limits, and mandates cooling-off periods

statute_links.name: Austin City Code § 25-2-781 - Payday Lending Ordinance
statute_links.description: Local ordinance limiting payday loan amounts to $1,500 and restricting rollovers for CAB loans

statute_links.name: Dallas City Code § 51A.3 - Payday Lending Ordinance
statute_links.description: Local ordinance capping payday loans at $500 with mandatory 90-day waiting period between consecutive loans

statute_links.name: Texas Property Code § 41 - Homestead Exemptions
statute_links.description: Establishes homestead property exemptions and protections for veterans and military service members

TX.recent_changes: No major state-level lending or credit repair legislation enacted in Texas in 2024-2025. However, continued local regulatory activity by major cities (Austin, Dallas, San Antonio, Houston) regarding CAB lending restrictions remains active. The Texas Office of Consumer Credit Commissioner continues to monitor and enforce existing CAB regulations and has provided guidance on compliance with federal Military Lending Act requirements for lenders serving military personnel stationed in Texas.

UT.name: Utah
UT.usury_cap: No usury cap for written agreements; payday loans legal with no rate cap
UT.consumer_protection_agency: Utah Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
key_regulations: Utah has no usury cap for written agreements, allowing unlimited interest rates on payday loans.
Payday loans are not capped by amount, only by borrower ability to repay.
Lenders must be registered with the Department of Financial Institutions.
The Utah Consumer Credit Code provides basic lending protections.
UT.consumer_rights_summary: Utah is one of the most permissive states for payday lending, with no usury cap on written agreements and no dollar cap on loan amounts. Consumers should exercise extreme caution as APRs can be very high. Complaints can be filed with the Department of Financial Institutions or the Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit services organizations must provide a written contract clearly stating all services to be performed, total cost, and timeline
Prohibited from charging or collecting fees until services are actually performed
Must disclose to consumers their right to obtain credit reports directly from credit bureaus at no charge
Prohibited from making false or misleading representations about credit repair outcomes or timeline
Must provide a 5-day cancellation period during which consumers can cancel the contract without penalty
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Utah Department of Financial Institutions

personal_loans.rate_cap: No specific usury cap for written agreements
personal_loans.notes: Governed by Utah Consumer Credit Code (Utah Code § 70C-1 et seq.); lenders must be licensed with Department of Financial Institutions

payday_loans.rate_cap: No rate cap; unlimited fees and interest rates permitted
payday_loans.notes: Utah has no usury cap on written agreements, making it one of the most permissive states for payday lending; lenders must be registered with Department of Financial Institutions; no maximum loan amount or term limit; no statewide database tracking

title_loans.rate_cap: No rate cap; unlimited interest rates permitted
title_loans.notes: Title loans are legal in Utah with no rate cap; subject to Consumer Credit Code registration requirements

installment_loans.rate_cap: No specific usury cap for written agreements
installment_loans.notes: Regulated under Utah Consumer Credit Code (§ 70C); lenders must comply with licensing requirements and disclosure obligations

mortgage.notes: Mortgages in Utah are subject to Utah's Residential Mortgage Practices Act (Utah Code § 61-2c-1 et seq.); mortgage lenders and loan originators must be licensed; foreclosure is non-judicial (power-of-sale) for mortgages with a power-of-sale clause; judicial foreclosure available when power-of-sale provision absent; 120+ days notice required before foreclosure; borrowers have redemption rights; licensing administered by Department of Financial Institutions

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 3953) applies to active-duty service members and their dependents in Utah, capping Annual Percentage Rates (MAPR) at 36% for covered borrowing. This applies to payday loans, title loans, installment loans, and certain other consumer credit products. Covered borrowers must receive specific disclosures and protections.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) protects active-duty service members in Utah by capping interest rates at 6% on pre-service debts, providing foreclosure and eviction protections, and allowing lease termination with 30 days' notice. Creditors must reduce interest rates upon receipt of military orders.
state_specific_protections: Utah offers property tax exemptions for disabled veterans and their surviving spouses under Utah Code § 59-2-103
Veterans may qualify for certain housing benefits and loan programs through the Utah Division of Veterans Affairs
State licensing regulations require lenders to be aware of MLA and SCRA protections; violations can result in license suspension or revocation
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Utah is a VA loan-friendly state with no state-specific restrictions on VA-guaranteed loans; property is freely transferable between VA loan borrowers; property tax exemptions available for disabled veterans; Utah Housing Finance Agency administers affordable housing programs available to veterans; no state property transfer taxes on VA-financed properties
veteran_protections.military_installations: Hill Air Force Base (Ogden area); Naval Base Kitsap Detachment (regional presence); Fort Douglas (historic; primarily administrative/educational presence); significant veteran population throughout the state

complaint_resources.name: Utah Department of Financial Institutions
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints against licensed payday lenders, title loan lenders, mortgage lenders, and credit repair organizations; enforces licensing requirements and consumer protection regulations

complaint_resources.name: Utah Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Investigates consumer complaints related to fraud, deceptive practices, and violations of consumer protection laws; accepts complaints about payday lenders and other creditors

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about payday lenders, title loan lenders, mortgage lenders, and other financial institutions; investigates and enforces federal consumer financial protection laws

complaint_resources.name: Better Business Bureau (Utah)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about payday lenders and credit services organizations; provides business ratings and dispute resolution services

statute_links.name: Utah Consumer Credit Code
statute_links.description: Comprehensive state law governing consumer credit transactions, including lending practices, disclosure requirements, and remedies

statute_links.name: Utah Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair companies, including bonding requirements, fee restrictions, cancellation rights, and prohibited practices

statute_links.name: Utah Department of Financial Institutions Licensing Information
statute_links.description: Information on payday lender registration, title loan lender licensing, and other consumer lending regulation

statute_links.name: Utah Residential Mortgage Practices Act
statute_links.description: Regulates mortgage lenders, loan originators, and servicing practices; establishes licensing requirements

statute_links.name: Utah Code § 57-1-1 et seq. (Foreclosure Procedures)
statute_links.description: Governs non-judicial and judicial foreclosure procedures, notice requirements, and borrower protections

UT.recent_changes: No significant legislative changes affecting consumer lending or credit repair were enacted in 2024-2025. Utah's lending laws have remained relatively stable, maintaining its permissive regulatory approach with no usury cap on written agreements and continued focus on lender licensing through the Department of Financial Institutions.

VT.name: Vermont
VT.usury_cap: 12% general usury; 18% for licensed lenders; payday lending banned
VT.consumer_protection_agency: Vermont Attorney General Consumer Assistance Program
key_regulations: Payday lending is effectively banned through rate caps (18% for licensed lenders).
The general usury cap is 12% for consumer loans.
Licensed lender rates capped at 18%, making payday lending impractical.
The Vermont Consumer Protection Act provides strong anti-predatory lending protections.
VT.consumer_rights_summary: Vermont effectively bans payday lending through its 18% rate cap for licensed lenders. The state has one of the lowest unbanked rates in the nation. Consumers can file complaints with the Department of Financial Regulation or the Attorney General's Consumer Assistance Program.

key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide written contract clearly stating services, costs, timeline, and consumer rights before any payment is collected
Prohibition on making any representations or claims about ability to improve credit record that cannot be substantiated
Prohibition on advising consumers to dispute accurate information on credit reports
Prohibition on charging or collecting fees until services are fully performed; no advance payment allowed except as security deposit not exceeding 25% of agreed fees
Consumers have unconditional right to cancel contract within 3 business days of signing without penalty or obligation
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Vermont Department of Financial Regulation

personal_loans.rate_cap: 12% APR for consumer loans; 18% APR for loans made by licensed lenders
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans are regulated under Vermont's usury statutes (6 V.S.A. § 226 et seq.). Licensed lenders may charge up to 18% to remain competitive; unlicensed lenders capped at 12%.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Effectively banned through 18% rate cap for licensed lenders
payday_loans.notes: Vermont does not permit payday lending. The 18% rate cap for licensed lenders makes traditional payday loan lending economically impractical. No specific payday loan statute exists; regulation occurs through general usury and licensing laws.

title_loans.rate_cap: Subject to 18% rate cap for licensed lenders
title_loans.notes: Title lending is not specifically authorized in Vermont statutes. Vehicle title loans would be subject to general usury caps and licensing requirements, making them impractical.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 12% APR general cap; 18% APR for licensed lenders
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are permitted under Vermont law and subject to the same usury caps as personal loans. Governed by Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 6, § 226 et seq.

mortgage.notes: Vermont mortgages are governed by Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 6 (creditors and debtors) and tit. 12 (property). Foreclosure is judicial (requires court action). Mortgagees must comply with federal Truth in Lending Act (TILA), Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA), and Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA). Vermont requires license for mortgage lenders/brokers under Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 6, § 4801 et seq.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 USCA § 987) applies to all active duty service members in Vermont. The law caps annual percentage rates (MAPR) at 36% on covered borrowing products and prohibits certain predatory practices including mandatory arbitration clauses, negative amortization, and balloon payments. Lenders must provide clear disclosures of all fees and terms.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 USCA § 3951 et seq.) provides active duty service members in Vermont with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, protection from foreclosure and eviction, lease termination rights, and stay of legal proceedings. Service members must provide notice of military status to creditors to receive protections.
state_specific_protections: Vermont recognizes military service in tax law and provides property tax exemptions for service-connected disabled veterans (Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 32, § 3802)
Vermont housing authority programs prioritize veteran access to affordable housing; contact Vermont Housing Finance Agency for veteran lending programs
State consumer protection laws under Vermont Consumer Protection Act (Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 6, § 4601 et seq.) apply equally to protect all consumers including service members from predatory lending
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Vermont Housing Finance Agency administers VA loan programs and state-specific veteran housing programs. Veterans may qualify for property tax exemptions on homestead properties. Contact VHFA at (802) 654-0505 for VA loan and veteran housing benefit information.
veteran_protections.military_installations: No major active duty military installations. Vermont is home to Vermont Army National Guard facilities and air defense units; primary focus areas include service members and guard/reserve members using consumer lending services.

complaint_resources.name: Vermont Department of Financial Regulation
complaint_resources.description: Regulates banks, credit unions, lending institutions, mortgage lenders, and credit repair organizations. Handles complaints about licensed lenders and credit service violations.

complaint_resources.name: Vermont Attorney General - Consumer Assistance Program
complaint_resources.description: Handles consumer complaints about unfair/deceptive practices, predatory lending, identity theft, and fraud. Can assist with complaint filing and consumer education.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal agency handling complaints about all types of consumer financial products and services including lending, credit reporting, and credit repair.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints about identity theft, fraud, and unfair business practices by credit repair companies and lenders.

complaint_resources.name: Better Business Bureau of Northern New England
complaint_resources.description: Accreditation and complaint resolution for businesses in Vermont; useful for filing disputes with BBB-accredited lenders and credit services.

statute_links.name: Vermont Usury Law (Creditors and Debtors)
statute_links.description: Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 6, § 226 et seq. - Establishes 12% general usury cap and 18% cap for licensed lenders; governs interest rates on consumer loans.

statute_links.name: Vermont Credit Services Organizations Act
statute_links.description: Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 6, § 4651-4670 - Regulates credit repair companies including bonding requirements, contract provisions, fee restrictions, and consumer rights.

statute_links.name: Vermont Consumer Protection Act
statute_links.description: Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 6, § 4601 et seq. - Prohibits unfair, unconscionable, and deceptive trade practices; applies broadly to all consumer lending and credit transactions.

statute_links.name: Vermont Mortgage Lender and Broker Licensing Law
statute_links.description: Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 6, § 4801 et seq. - Establishes licensing, education, and conduct requirements for mortgage lenders and brokers; implements federal lending standards.

statute_links.name: Vermont Foreclosure and Judicial Sale Law
statute_links.description: Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 12, § 4531 et seq. - Establishes judicial foreclosure procedures; Vermont requires court action for mortgage foreclosure.

statute_links.name: Vermont Property Tax Exemptions for Veterans
statute_links.description: Vt. Stat. Ann. tit. 32, § 3802 - Provides property tax exemptions for service-connected disabled veterans and their surviving spouses.

VT.recent_changes: No significant changes to Vermont's core lending or credit repair statutes in 2024-2025. Vermont maintains its strong consumer protections through the 12% general usury cap and effective payday lending ban. The state continues enforcement focus on predatory lending compliance through the Department of Financial Regulation and Attorney General's office.

VA.name: Virginia
VA.usury_cap: 36% APR cap on consumer loans (Fairness in Lending Act, 2020); 12% general usury
VA.consumer_protection_agency: Virginia Attorney General Consumer Protection Section
key_regulations: The Fairness in Lending Act (2020) capped all consumer loans at 36% APR plus a maintenance fee.
Replaced the previous open-end credit and payday loan frameworks.
Short-term loans limited to $2,500 with a maximum 24-month term.
The Virginia Consumer Protection Act provides additional protections against predatory lending.
VA.consumer_rights_summary: Virginia enacted the Fairness in Lending Act in 2020, capping consumer loans at 36% APR and reforming the payday and title lending industry. Short-term loans are limited to $2,500 with reasonable terms. Consumers can file complaints with the Bureau of Financial Institutions or the Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide a written contract before providing services, clearly stating all terms and conditions
Organizations must disclose all consumer rights under federal law, including rights under the Fair Credit Reporting Act and Fair Debt Collection Practices Act
Upfront payment for credit repair services is prohibited; payment must be contingent on actual results
Consumers have the right to cancel a contract within 5 business days without penalty or obligation
Credit repair organizations cannot charge for disputing inaccurate information on credit reports or claim they can remove accurate negative information
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Virginia State Corporation Commission, Bureau of Financial Institutions

personal_loans.rate_cap: 36% APR (Fairness in Lending Act, 2020) plus a maintenance fee
personal_loans.notes: Consumer loans are capped at 36% APR under Virginia's Fairness in Lending Act. Traditional installment loans are permissible; lenders must be licensed by the Bureau of Financial Institutions.

payday_loans.rate_cap: 36% APR cap (Fairness in Lending Act, 2020); treated as short-term consumer loans
payday_loans.notes: Payday loans are heavily restricted and recharacterized as short-term consumer loans under the 2020 Fairness in Lending Act. Maximum loan amount is $2,500 with a maximum 24-month (720-day) term. Lenders must report to Virginia's loan database to prevent multiple simultaneous loans. Only one outstanding short-term loan permitted per consumer.

title_loans.notes: Title loans are prohibited in Virginia as of the enactment of the Fairness in Lending Act in 2020.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 36% APR (Fairness in Lending Act, 2020)
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are permissible under Virginia law and are subject to the same 36% APR cap as other consumer loans. Loans must be for a reasonable term and lenders must be licensed by the Bureau of Financial Institutions.

mortgage.notes: Virginia mortgages are governed by Va. Code Ann. § 35.1 et seq. Virginia is a non-judicial foreclosure state, allowing lenders to foreclose through the power of sale without court proceedings if authorized in the deed of trust. Judicial foreclosure is also available. Borrowers have redemption rights and must receive proper notice. Residential mortgage lenders and brokers must be licensed by the Bureau of Financial Institutions under Va. Code Ann. § 6.2-1606 et seq.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 3953) applies nationwide and caps the Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) at 36% for active-duty service members and their dependents on consumer credit. Virginia's 36% APR cap under the Fairness in Lending Act aligns with and provides equivalent protections to the federal MLA.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) provides active-duty service members with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, protections against foreclosure and eviction, lease termination rights without penalty, and extended time to comply with legal obligations during active service.
state_specific_protections: Virginia enacted the Fairness in Lending Act (2020) which caps all consumer loans at 36% APR, providing protections consistent with the Military Lending Act for active-duty military personnel
Virginia Code § 34.1 et seq. provides property tax exemptions for veterans with service-connected disabilities rated at 100% by the VA
Virginia offers the Virginia Housing Development Authority (VHDA) with dedicated VA loan programs and down payment assistance for eligible veterans
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: The Virginia Housing Development Authority (VHDA) administers VA loan programs including conventional mortgages with favorable terms for eligible veterans. Virginia provides property tax relief through an exemption for real property owned by veterans with 100% service-connected disability ratings. The state also offers the Virginia War Related Debt Relief Program for certain veterans and their families.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Joint Base Langley-Eustis (Hampton), Naval Station Norfolk (Norfolk), Naval Submarine Base Kings Bay (adjacent in Georgia), Marine Corps Base Quantico (Triangle), Fort Belvoir (Fairfax County), Fort Lee (Petersburg), Naval Weapons Station Yorktown (Yorktown)

complaint_resources.name: Virginia Bureau of Financial Institutions
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints against licensed lenders, credit repair organizations, mortgage companies, and other financial service providers. Enforces the Fairness in Lending Act and credit-related regulations.

complaint_resources.name: Virginia Attorney General Consumer Protection Section
complaint_resources.description: Accepts complaints about predatory lending, unfair lending practices, credit repair fraud, and other consumer finance violations. Can take enforcement action against violators.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal regulator handling complaints about consumer financial products and services, including loans, credit reporting, and lending discrimination.

complaint_resources.name: Virginia Fair Housing Office
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints of lending discrimination based on protected characteristics including race, color, national origin, religion, sex, disability, and familial status.

statute_links.name: Virginia Fairness in Lending Act (2020)
statute_links.description: Comprehensive 2020 legislation capping consumer loans at 36% APR, restricting payday loans to $2,500 with maximum 24-month terms, and prohibiting title loans.

statute_links.name: Virginia Consumer Protection Act
statute_links.description: Protects consumers against unfair and deceptive practices in consumer transactions, including lending and credit services.

statute_links.name: Virginia Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair companies, requiring licensing, bonding, written contracts, and prohibiting upfront fees.

statute_links.name: Virginia Code § 6.2-1606 et seq. (Mortgage Lender and Broker Licensing)
statute_links.description: Establishes licensing and regulatory requirements for mortgage lenders, brokers, and loan officers in Virginia.

statute_links.name: Virginia Code § 35.1 et seq. (Property and Conveyances - Mortgages)
statute_links.description: Governs mortgage creation, enforcement, non-judicial foreclosure through power of sale, and borrower protections.

VA.recent_changes: The Fairness in Lending Act (2020) represents Virginia's most significant recent consumer lending reform, substantially restructuring payday and short-term lending regulations with a 36% APR cap and eliminating title loans. No major additional lending or credit repair legislation was enacted in 2024-2025, though regulatory enforcement of the 2020 Act continues.

WA.name: Washington
WA.usury_cap: 12% general usury; payday loans capped at $700 with tiered fees (15% on first $500)
WA.consumer_protection_agency: Washington Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
key_regulations: Payday loans capped at $700 or 30% of gross monthly income, whichever is less.
Maximum fee of 15% on first $500 and 10% above $500.
Borrowers limited to eight payday loans per 12-month period.
After the eighth loan, borrower must be offered a no-cost installment plan.
WA.consumer_rights_summary: Washington allows payday lending with a $700 cap, tiered fee structure, and a limit of eight loans per year. After the eighth loan, borrowers must be offered a no-cost installment plan. The Department of Financial Institutions regulates consumer lenders, and complaints can be filed with DFI or the Attorney General.

key_provisions: Credit repair companies must provide written contract detailing all services, fees, and cancellation rights before charging fees
Prohibition on charging fees before services are provided or promised results are achieved
Mandatory 5-day cancellation period during which consumer may cancel without penalty
Credit repair companies must disclose that consumers have the right to dispute items with credit bureaus directly at no cost
Prohibition on making false or misleading claims about ability to improve credit scores or remove accurate negative information
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Washington Department of Financial Institutions

personal_loans.rate_cap: 12% APR general usury cap
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans regulated under Washington general usury law (RCW 19.52). Lenders must be licensed by DFI. Rates may exceed 12% for certain consumer lenders under specific conditions.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Maximum fee of 15% on first $500 and 10% on amounts above $500
payday_loans.notes: Payday loans capped at $700 or 30% of gross monthly income (whichever is less). Borrowers limited to 8 loans per 12-month period. After 8th loan, lender must offer no-cost installment plan. Database tracking required to prevent lending violations. Regulated by Department of Financial Institutions under RCW 31.45.

title_loans.notes: Motor vehicle title loans are prohibited in Washington state.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 12% APR general usury cap applies; consumer lenders may charge higher rates under RCW 31.04 licensing provisions
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans regulated under Washington Consumer Loan Act (RCW 31.04). Lenders must be licensed. No-cost installment plans required after 8 payday loans in 12 months.

mortgage.notes: Washington mortgages regulated under RCW 61.24 (non-judicial foreclosure). Lenders must be licensed by DFI. Non-judicial foreclosure permitted with notice requirements. TRID/Dodd-Frank federal rules apply. Washington Homeownership Preservation Act provides protections for owner-occupied residential property.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) caps Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) at 36% for servicemembers and their dependents. This applies to all consumer credit products in Washington. Covered borrowers cannot be charged fees, interest rates, or other charges exceeding 36% MAPR.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) caps interest on pre-service debts at 6% during active duty. SCRA provides foreclosure protections, lease termination rights, and protection from default judgment without court appearance. Servicemembers must provide notice of military service to creditors.
state_specific_protections: Washington Property Tax Exemption for Disabled Veterans (RCW 84.36.379-84.36.389) provides property tax exemptions for veterans with service-connected disabilities rated by the VA
Washington State Department of Veterans Affairs provides counseling and assistance with credit issues, debt management, and financial hardship programs for veterans
Servicemembers and veterans are eligible for reduced-rate refinancing and loan modification programs through state-supported credit unions and community banks
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Washington Housing Finance Commission administers VA loan guarantee programs. No state property tax on veterans' homestead exemptions in certain counties. VA home loan funding fee waivers available for disabled veterans rated 0% or higher by VA.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort Lewis (Joint Base Lewis-McChord - JBLM), Naval Base Kitsap, Naval Station Bremerton, Whidbey Island Naval Air Station, Fairchild Air Force Base (Spokane area)

complaint_resources.name: Washington Department of Financial Institutions - Consumer Complaint Unit
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints against licensed lenders, credit repair companies, and financial institutions. Enforces payday lending regulations, usury laws, and credit services organization requirements.

complaint_resources.name: Washington Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Investigates unfair/deceptive lending practices, payday loan violations, credit repair fraud, and predatory lending. Accepts complaints and enforces consumer protection laws.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal consumer complaint portal for lending, credit reporting, credit repair, and other financial products. Handles complaints nationwide.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Reports credit repair scams, identity theft, predatory lending, and other consumer fraud.

complaint_resources.name: Washington Department of Veterans Affairs
complaint_resources.description: Provides support and resources for veterans experiencing financial hardship, predatory lending, and credit issues.

statute_links.name: Washington Consumer Loan Act
statute_links.description: Governs installment loans and consumer lending rates; establishes 12% usury cap and DFI licensing requirements

statute_links.name: Washington Payday Loan Regulations
statute_links.description: Establishes payday loan caps ($700), fee structure (15% on first $500, 10% above), 8-loan limit, and installment plan requirements

statute_links.name: Washington Usury Law
statute_links.description: Sets general 12% APR usury cap for consumer loans and establishes licensing exceptions for consumer lenders

statute_links.name: Washington Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair companies; requires registration, bonding, written contracts, and prohibits upfront fees

statute_links.name: Washington Mortgage Foreclosure Act
statute_links.description: Governs non-judicial foreclosure procedures, notice requirements, and borrower protections in mortgage default
