statute_links.name: Washington Homeownership Preservation Act
statute_links.description: Provides foreclosure prevention assistance and protections for homeowners facing foreclosure; regulates foreclosure consultant practices

statute_links.name: Washington Department of Financial Institutions Consumer Lending Rules
statute_links.description: Administrative rules and licensing requirements for consumer lenders, payday loan providers, and credit services organizations

statute_links.name: Washington Veterans Property Tax Exemption
statute_links.description: Provides property tax exemptions for disabled veterans with service-connected disabilities

WA.recent_changes: Washington enacted significant payday lending reforms in 2019 (HB 1517) reducing the maximum loan amount from $1,500 to $700 and implementing the eight-loan annual cap with mandatory no-cost installment plan option. No major new legislation affecting consumer lending was enacted in 2024-2025.

WV.name: West Virginia
WV.usury_cap: 8% general usury; 31% for supervised consumer loans; payday lending banned since 2006
WV.consumer_protection_agency: West Virginia Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
WV.licensing_board: West Virginia Division of Financial Institutions
key_regulations: Payday lending banned since 2006 when the enabling statute expired and was not renewed.
The general usury cap is 8% for most consumer loans.
Licensed supervised consumer lenders can charge up to 31%.
The West Virginia Consumer Credit and Protection Act provides comprehensive consumer protections.
WV.consumer_rights_summary: West Virginia banned payday lending in 2006 when the authorizing statute expired. The state maintains an 8% general usury cap with higher caps for licensed lenders. Despite being one of the poorest states, the payday ban protects consumers from high-cost debt traps. Complaints can be filed with the Division of Financial Institutions or the Attorney General.

credit_repair_laws.state_statute: West Virginia Credit Services Organization Act
key_provisions: Credit repair organizations must provide written contracts detailing all services, fees, and cancellation terms before any payment is accepted
Prohibits misrepresentation of services or guaranteed results in credit repair
Requires clear disclosure of consumer rights under federal credit repair laws
Organizations must comply with federal Credit Repair Organizations Act (CROA) standards
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: West Virginia Division of Financial Institutions

personal_loans.rate_cap: 8% APR (general usury cap); up to 31% APR for licensed supervised consumer lenders
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans are governed by the West Virginia Consumer Credit and Protection Act. The 8% general usury cap applies unless the lender holds a supervised lender license, which allows rates up to 31%.

payday_loans.rate_cap: Banned
payday_loans.notes: Payday lending has been banned in West Virginia since 2006 when the enabling statute expired and was not renewed. This protects consumers from high-cost debt traps.

title_loans.rate_cap: Not applicable; effectively banned under consumer protection statutes
title_loans.notes: Title loans are not explicitly authorized under West Virginia law and are treated similarly to payday loans as predatory lending practices.

installment_loans.rate_cap: 8% APR general cap; up to 31% APR for licensed supervised consumer lenders
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are governed by W.Va. Code § 46A-2-101 et seq. (West Virginia Consumer Credit and Protection Act). All consumer installment loans must comply with disclosure requirements under this act.

mortgage.notes: West Virginia mortgages are subject to the Consumer Credit and Protection Act. Foreclosures are primarily judicial proceedings conducted through circuit courts. State law requires pre-foreclosure notice and right to cure. Licensing requirements for mortgage lenders are enforced by the West Virginia Division of Financial Institutions. First mortgages are generally exempt from usury limits under W.Va. Code § 46A-2-127.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The federal Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) applies in West Virginia, capping the Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) at 36% for active-duty service members and their dependents. This applies to payday loans, vehicle title loans, installment loans, and other covered loans. The law also prohibits certain terms and requires creditors to verify military status.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3901 et seq.) provides federal protections including: capping pre-service debts at 6% interest, protecting service members from foreclosure and eviction during active duty, allowing lease termination with 30 days' notice, and requiring creditor consent before obtaining judgment. These protections apply to all active-duty service members regardless of state of residence.
state_specific_protections: West Virginia provides property tax exemptions for disabled veterans and surviving spouses under W.Va. Code § 11-1A-2, reducing property tax burden for qualifying veterans
The state honors federal MLA protections and works with creditors to ensure compliance with the 36% MAPR cap for service members
West Virginia offers the Veteran Housing Assistance Program providing down payment assistance and favorable loan terms for eligible veterans purchasing homes
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: West Virginia recognizes VA loans and property tax exemptions for qualifying veterans. The state does not impose additional interest rate caps on VA-backed mortgages beyond federal requirements. Veterans purchasing homes in West Virginia may benefit from state property tax exemptions and assistance programs administered through the West Virginia Veterans Commission.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Major military installations in West Virginia include Camp Dawson (National Guard Training Center) in Preston County and various recruiting and reserve centers throughout the state. No active-duty DoD installations, but significant National Guard and Reserve presence.

complaint_resources.name: West Virginia Division of Financial Institutions
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints regarding payday lenders, supervised consumer lenders, credit services organizations, mortgage lenders, and other financial institutions. Enforces lending regulations and usury laws.

complaint_resources.name: West Virginia Attorney General Consumer Protection Division
complaint_resources.description: Investigates consumer complaints involving unfair, deceptive, or abusive practices by lenders and credit repair companies. Can pursue legal action on behalf of consumers.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Accepts federal complaints about credit cards, mortgages, consumer loans, credit reporting, and other consumer financial products and services.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints regarding identity theft, credit repair fraud, and other consumer protection violations.

statute_links.name: West Virginia Consumer Credit and Protection Act
statute_links.description: Comprehensive consumer credit law governing consumer loans, installment sales, truth in lending disclosures, and general usury limits (8% cap).

statute_links.name: West Virginia Credit Services Organization Act
statute_links.description: Regulates credit repair companies, requiring contracts, prohibiting upfront fees, and mandating 3-day cancellation rights.

statute_links.name: West Virginia Supervised Lenders Law
statute_links.description: Governs licensed supervised consumer lenders, allowing rates up to 31% APR with appropriate licensing and regulation.

statute_links.name: West Virginia Property Tax Exemptions for Veterans
statute_links.description: Provides property tax relief for disabled veterans and surviving spouses of deceased veterans.

statute_links.name: West Virginia Code § 46A-2-127 (Usury Exemptions)
statute_links.description: Exempts first mortgages, loans on agricultural land, and certain other loans from the general 8% usury cap.

WV.recent_changes: No significant changes to West Virginia consumer lending or credit repair laws in 2024-2025. The state's payday lending ban remains in effect since 2006, and the usury structure has remained stable with the 8% general cap and 31% supervised lender cap continuing to apply.

WI.name: Wisconsin
WI.usury_cap: No general usury cap for licensed lenders; payday loans legal with no rate cap
WI.consumer_protection_agency: Wisconsin Attorney General Consumer Protection Bureau
key_regulations: Wisconsin has no usury cap for licensed lenders, allowing unlimited payday loan rates.
Payday loans are governed by the Wisconsin Consumer Act but without rate caps.
Licensed lenders must disclose APR and fees but are not limited by them.
The Wisconsin Consumer Act provides basic protections but does not cap payday rates.
WI.consumer_rights_summary: Wisconsin is one of the most permissive states for payday lending, with no rate cap for licensed lenders. Effective APRs can exceed 500%. The Department of Financial Institutions requires licensing but does not limit rates. Consumers should exercise extreme caution and can file complaints with DFI or the Attorney General.

credit_repair_laws.state_statute: Wisconsin Consumer Act - Credit Services Organization provisions
key_provisions: Credit services organizations must provide written contract before any services rendered, with clear disclosure of services, costs, and timeline
Prohibition on collection of fees before services are delivered and results achieved for the consumer
Must disclose consumer's right to obtain credit report directly from credit bureaus at no cost
Three-business-day cancellation right allowing consumers to void contract without penalty
Prohibition on misleading or deceptive advertising regarding credit repair services
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Wisconsin Department of Financial Institutions

personal_loans.rate_cap: No specific cap for licensed lenders under Wisconsin Consumer Act; subject to general contract law
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans by licensed lenders are not subject to a usury cap in Wisconsin. Lenders must comply with Wisconsin Consumer Act disclosure requirements.

payday_loans.rate_cap: No rate cap for licensed lenders; unlimited APR permitted
payday_loans.notes: Wisconsin has no usury cap for licensed payday lenders. Effective APRs commonly exceed 500%. Licensed lenders must disclose APR and fees under Wisconsin Consumer Act but are not limited by rate caps. No state database tracks outstanding loans. Department of Financial Institutions requires licensing.

title_loans.rate_cap: No specific rate cap; subject to general Wisconsin lending law
title_loans.notes: Title loans are permitted in Wisconsin. Licensed lenders must comply with general Wisconsin Consumer Act disclosure requirements but no rate caps apply.

installment_loans.rate_cap: No specific cap; subject to Wisconsin Consumer Act requirements
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans are legal and governed by Wisconsin Consumer Act (Wis. Stat. § 427.101 et seq.), which requires disclosure of APR, finance charges, and payment terms but does not impose rate caps.

mortgage.notes: Wisconsin mortgages are governed by Wis. Stat. § 704.01 et seq. Foreclosures are judicial (require court action). Licensed mortgage lenders and brokers must be registered with the Wisconsin Department of Financial Institutions. Residential mortgage lender licensing requirements found in Wis. Stat. § 224.71 et seq. Wisconsin requires compliance with federal TRID (TILA-RESPA Integrated Disclosure) and provides statutory protections including notice requirements before foreclosure.

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) applies in Wisconsin, capping the annual percentage rate (including all fees and charges) at 36% MAPR for covered borrowers (active duty service members and their dependents). This applies to payday loans, title loans, installment loans, and other consumer credit. Wisconsin lenders offering credit to military members must comply with MLA disclosure and rate cap requirements.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3953) provides covered service members with a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts, protection from foreclosure and eviction during active duty, lease termination rights, and other financial protections. SCRA applies nationwide including Wisconsin and does not require state action.
state_specific_protections: Wisconsin does not have state-specific military lending rate caps; federal MLA protections (36% MAPR) are the applicable standard
Wisconsin provides property tax exemptions for veterans with service-connected disabilities (Wis. Stat. § 70.38), which may provide indirect financial relief
Wisconsin Housing and Economic Development Authority (WHEDA) offers VA loan programs and down payment assistance for qualifying veterans
Wisconsin Department of Veterans Affairs provides financial counseling and can assist veterans in identifying predatory lending practices
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Wisconsin Housing and Economic Development Authority (WHEDA) administers VA loan programs with favorable terms for veterans. State law provides partial property tax exemption for veterans with service-connected disabilities rated 50% or more (up to $65,000 of home value exempt, or $120,000 for 100% disabled veterans). Veterans may also be eligible for state foreclosure prevention assistance programs.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Fort McCoy (La Crosse County) is a major U.S. Army Reserve and National Guard installation in Wisconsin. Significant military personnel presence supports MLA enforcement focus in surrounding communities.

complaint_resources.name: Wisconsin Department of Financial Institutions
complaint_resources.description: Regulates licensed lenders, mortgage lenders, credit services organizations, and other financial service providers. Handles complaints about unfair lending practices, payday loans, title loans, and licensing violations.

complaint_resources.name: Wisconsin Attorney General Consumer Protection Bureau
complaint_resources.description: Enforces Wisconsin Consumer Act and consumer protection laws. Investigates complaints about deceptive lending practices, credit repair fraud, and other consumer protection violations.

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal agency handling complaints about consumer financial products and services, including payday loans, title loans, credit repair services, and violations of federal consumer protection laws.

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Handles complaints about fraud, deceptive practices, and unfair methods in consumer finance, including predatory lending and credit repair scams.

complaint_resources.name: Wisconsin Department of Veterans Affairs
complaint_resources.description: Assists veterans with financial and legal issues, including predatory lending complaints and access to veteran-specific financial assistance programs.

WI.recent_changes: No significant changes to Wisconsin's payday lending or credit repair laws in 2024-2025. Wisconsin maintains one of the most permissive regulatory environments for payday lending in the nation with no rate caps for licensed lenders.

WY.name: Wyoming
WY.usury_cap: No usury cap for written agreements; payday loans legal with $30 per $100 fee cap
WY.consumer_protection_agency: Wyoming Attorney General Consumer Protection Unit
key_regulations: Payday loans governed by the Wyoming Uniform Consumer Credit Code with $30 per $100 fee cap.
No general usury cap for written agreements in Wyoming.
Maximum payday loan term is one calendar month.
Title loans also permitted with limited regulation.
WY.consumer_rights_summary: Wyoming allows payday lending with a $30 per $100 fee cap under the Uniform Consumer Credit Code. The state has no general usury cap for written agreements. Consumers can file complaints with the Division of Banking or the Attorney General's Consumer Protection Unit.

key_provisions: Credit services organizations must provide consumers with a written contract describing all services, fees, and cancellation rights before any payment is collected
Organizations must disclose that consumers have the right to obtain credit reports directly from credit reporting agencies at no cost
Prohibited from charging fees until promised services are actually performed
Cannot guarantee removal of accurate negative information from credit reports
Must comply with the federal Credit Repair Organizations Act (CROA) requirements
credit_repair_laws.registration_agency: Wyoming Division of Banking (oversight and enforcement)

personal_loans.rate_cap: No specific cap for written agreements; governed by Wyoming Uniform Consumer Credit Code
personal_loans.notes: Personal loans are legal and largely unregulated absent written usury cap; lenders may charge any rate agreed to in writing

payday_loans.rate_cap: $30 per $100 borrowed
payday_loans.notes: Wyoming permits payday lending under the Uniform Consumer Credit Code; one calendar month maximum term; no loan amount cap; no state-level database tracking requirement

title_loans.notes: Title loans are permitted in Wyoming with limited regulatory oversight; interest rates and terms are largely unregulated

installment_loans.rate_cap: No specific cap for written agreements
installment_loans.notes: Installment loans governed by Wyoming Uniform Consumer Credit Code; no general usury cap applies to written agreements

mortgage.notes: Wyoming is a non-judicial foreclosure state; lenders may foreclose via power of sale without court involvement; Wyoming Department of Audit handles mortgage lender/servicer oversight; federally-chartered institutions regulated by federal agencies; state-chartered lenders regulated by Wyoming Division of Banking

veteran_protections.federal_mla_summary: The Military Lending Act (36 U.S.C. § 987) applies in Wyoming, capping the interest rate at 36% MAPR (military annual percentage rate) on covered borrowing for active-duty service members and their dependents. This applies to payday loans, title loans, installment loans, and other consumer credit unless exempted (e.g., mortgages, vehicle purchases financed by dealers). MAPR includes interest and certain fees but excludes some charges. Lenders must obtain a signed acknowledgment of the rate cap.
veteran_protections.federal_scra_summary: The Service Members Civil Relief Act (50 U.S.C. § 3951 et seq.) provides active-duty service members with: (1) a 6% interest rate cap on pre-service debts; (2) protection from foreclosure or repossession without court order while on active duty plus 9 months after; (3) the right to terminate certain leases; (4) extended statute of limitations tolling; and (5) other civil protections. Service members must provide military service documentation to creditors to invoke SCRA benefits.
state_specific_protections: Wyoming has no state-specific military lending usury cap; federal MLA 36% MAPR applies as the governing standard
Wyoming recognizes SCRA protections as federal law; state courts enforce SCRA defenses in foreclosure and collection actions
Property tax exemptions available for qualifying veterans and surviving spouses (Wyo. Stat. Ann. § 39-11-101 et seq.) providing significant property tax relief
Wyoming Veterans Commission provides benefits information, financial counseling resources, and referrals to military-focused financial assistance programs
veteran_protections.va_loan_notes: Wyoming recognizes VA-guaranteed loans; the state has no property-specific restrictions on VA loan use. Veterans may benefit from Wyoming's property tax exemption (up to $10,000 homestead exemption for qualifying disabled veterans) which reduces overall housing costs. VA loans financed through federally or state-regulated lenders; federal VA Loan Program administered by U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs.
veteran_protections.military_installations: Warren Air Force Base (ICBM base near Cheyenne) is a major military installation in Wyoming; also home to National Guard facilities. These bases are enforcement priority areas for Military Lending Act compliance.

complaint_resources.name: Wyoming Division of Banking
complaint_resources.description: Oversees state-chartered banks, credit unions, payday lenders, title lenders, and other financial service providers; handles licensing, examination, and consumer complaints related to lending and credit services

complaint_resources.name: Wyoming Attorney General — Consumer Protection Unit
complaint_resources.description: Investigates and prosecutes consumer protection violations including unfair/deceptive lending practices, credit repair fraud, and violations of the Uniform Consumer Credit Code; accepts complaints online and by mail

complaint_resources.name: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
complaint_resources.description: Federal agency that accepts complaints about payday loans, title loans, credit repair companies, banks, and other consumer financial products; can refer complaints to state regulators

complaint_resources.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
complaint_resources.description: Investigates credit repair fraud and deceptive lending practices; complements CFPB enforcement on credit repair organizations and certain lending practices

complaint_resources.name: Wyoming Veterans Commission
complaint_resources.description: Provides veteran benefits information, financial counseling referrals, and advocacy assistance for military-related financial disputes and lending issues

WY.recent_changes: No significant legislative changes to consumer lending or credit repair laws in Wyoming during 2024-2025. Wyoming's Uniform Consumer Credit Code and payday lending regulations remain stable; federal Military Lending Act and SCRA provisions continue to apply as primary consumer protections for active-duty service members.
